| What’s the problem? |  |  |  |
| Chassis Selection (has to have metabolic roadmap models for simulation purposes) Bacillus subtilis (more characterized; less EPS) Bacillus subtilis (less characterized; more EPS) Bacillus megaterium [Richardson Sarfo-Yeboah](mailto:rds10044@nyu.edu) Pseudomonas putida () [Naya Mansour](mailto:nm5098@nyu.edu) Non-pathogenic strain and well characterize KT2440 Pseudomonas aeruginosa () [Richardson Sarfo-Yeboah](mailto:rds10044@nyu.edu) | 4\. Pseudomonas putida KT2440: Pros: “The Superbug”  It produces high levels of Nicotinamide Adenine Dinucleotide Phosphate (NADPH). This molecule provides the specific chemical energy needed to build the Extracellular Polymeric Substances (EPS) while simultaneously fighting heat-induced cell damage. Using a signaling molecule called Cyclic Diguanosine Monophosphate (Cyclic-di-GMP), we can install a genetic switch. This allows us to "turn on" the sand-binding process exactly when we want by adding a trigger chemical. When levels of this molecule go up, the bacteria turn off flagella and start producing EPS. It uses a strategy called Priming. The bacteria stay "quiet" in good conditions but trigger a 360% increase in protective gene activity the moment it detects drought or heat. It is a model organism. It has a long history as a metabolic-engineering platform, a revised genome, genome-scale models, stable chromosomal integration options, CRISPR/CRISPRi tools and simulation like GEMs/ SEVA/ Fluxomics… It is non-pathogeni**c** and naturally lives in the Rhizosphere (the soil around plant roots), so it already grips onto sand and minerals. The strain has already been engineered to produce complex bioproducts like polyhydroxyalkanoates (PHAs) and rhamnolipids, which share similar metabolic pathways with the Extracellular Polymeric Substances (EPS). Interesting experiment: To make the biofilm production predictable, a team engineered a system where the bacteria only become sticky when a specific chemical (cyclohexanone) is added by inserting genes from *E. coli* into KT2440. When they added the chemical, they saw a 3-fold increase in biofilm formation, which is a massive jump for this specific strain.  Cons:  Standard strains stop growing at 41°C (critical temperature), they might become VBNC. We will have to use ALE (Adaptive Laboratory Evolution) or overexpress heat-shielding genes (*dnaK* and *rpoH).* Under extreme heat, the cells might enter a heat shock state. In this state, they stop producing EPS and spend their energy repairing their own proteins (dormancy risk defeats the point of using this chassis). The biofilm naturally produced by wild pseudomonas is not strong enough to stick to sand and stabilize it. We will also have to amplify gene clusters like *alg* and *bcs*. Under high heat and dryness, outer cells may die. When embedded in a self-produced EPS biofilm, Pseudomonas putida KT2440 becomes conditionally viable in desert-like temperatures, as the biofilm creates a hydrated and thermally buffered microenvironment. However, long-term survival and function still depend on preventing complete desiccation, meaning EPS production alone may not be sufficient without additional stress-protection strategies.  |  | Pseudomonas putida KT2440: [https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=PUHVLag4krQ](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=PUHVLag4krQ)  [https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4263515/\#sec10](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4263515/#sec10)  [https://journals.asm.org/doi/10.1128/jb.00136-24](https://journals.asm.org/doi/10.1128/jb.00136-24)  [https://www.irjmets.com/upload\_newfiles/irjmets71000071257/paper\_file/irjmets71000071257.pdf](https://www.irjmets.com/upload_newfiles/irjmets71000071257/paper_file/irjmets71000071257.pdf)  [https://scispace.com/pdf/influence-of-temperature-on-the-growth-of-pseudomonas-putida-4fa9rfgeo3.pdf](https://scispace.com/pdf/influence-of-temperature-on-the-growth-of-pseudomonas-putida-4fa9rfgeo3.pdf)    |
| What adhesive material do we use? What methods could we use? What are we targeting, X-silica interaction or X-other molecular components from the sand? Overexpression of EPS?  What is EPS made of? [Udaya Pandit](mailto:up2052@nyu.edu)[Edward Chen](mailto:sec9064@nyu.edu) Introducing new proteins? [Sid Kong](mailto:zk2185@nyu.edu) Delivery system? [Hoor Islam](mailto:hi2151@nyu.edu) Calcium carbonate as a binder [Richardson Sarfo-Yeboah](mailto:rds10044@nyu.edu)[Mahra Al Dhaheri](mailto:mma9420@nyu.edu)  Other existing mechanism [Sid Kong](mailto:zk2185@nyu.edu) Which is the best? | 1ai.  A biofilm consists of water and EPS (extracellular polymeric substances or exopolysaccharides?). The B. subtilis ECM (extracellular matrix) consists mostly of exopolysaccharide and TasA and Bs1A proteins  EPS acts as the adhesive element of the biofilm cells at the cell-to-cell interface, which is important for biofilm attachment Bs1A is a hydrophobin that forms a thin external hydrophobic layer and is the main factor that confers hydrophobic properties to biofilms TasA is a functional amyloid protein that forms fibers resistant to adverse physiochemical conditions that confer biofilms with stability  1bi. 1ci. 4 Delivery methods are possible:  a. Direct Inoculation  (liquid spray that contains cell suspension i.e water and additives and cell extract i.e EPS extract or CaCO3. This can be done using PVC pipes or drone sprays and can be done seasonally (e.g. April to May) to ensure optimal activity. This is easy to scale but it exposes bacteria to UV and desiccation) b. Encapsulation in sodium alginate beads (this will protect spores from UV radiation/ drying out quickly/ mechanical shear \+ we can do controlled release using this \+ sodium alginate beads are cheap, biocompatible and its very easy to acquire starting material for this) c. Coating (applying a thin layer e.g. alginate on the sand grains. This provides less protection compared to encapsulation but does allow rapid release) To avoid ammonia formation as a by-product, the system should move away from urease-based CaCO₃ production and instead rely on CO₂-driven pathways such as the carbonic anhydrase route or photosynthetic activity. In the carbonic anhydrase pathway, engineered bacteria catalyze the conversion of CO₂ into bicarbonate, which then reacts with Ca²⁺ to form calcium carbonate under mildly alkaline conditions. This approach eliminates nitrogen-containing intermediates, thereby preventing NH₃ release. A key requirement is maintaining a controlled alkaline microenvironment without disrupting the surrounding soil. This could be addressed through encapsulation strategies that include localized buffering systems, allowing pH to remain elevated only where precipitation is needed while minimizing broader environmental impact. In the photosynthetic pathway, microorganisms such as cyanobacteria consume CO₂ during photosynthesis, which naturally raises the local pH and promotes CaCO₃ precipitation. This method is similarly ammonia-free and self-sustaining in the presence of light and CO₂. However, it introduces constraints related to light availability and tends to favor the formation of calcite, the most thermodynamically stable and mechanically rigid polymorph of calcium carbonate. This is important because overly hard mineral formation may reduce flexibility in the sand matrix and lead to brittle structures rather than cohesive but adaptive binding. The mechanical properties of the precipitated material depend on both the polymorph (e.g., calcite vs. vaterite) and the crystal size. By regulating the availability of Ca²⁺ ions, it is possible to control crystal growth kinetics: lower ion concentrations and slower precipitation rates tend to produce smaller crystals, which result in a less rigid and more distributed binding network.  d. Lyapollization (bacteria freeze-dried into powder and then morning dew/ mist slowly absorbs into the bacteria and rehydrates it.  Bacteria will have much longer shelf life Can easily store \+ transport to remote/ bedouin areas (otherwise we need to store it at temp 2-8 degrees celsius) Natural timing: dew happens in the morning (lower temp) so bacteria isn’t stressed High humidity in  Abu Dhabi → seal with aluminum foils/ store with desiccants/ wax or paraffin coating  How long does the effect need to last? Are we looking to build a self-sustaining colony/ reproduces or is it repeated like once a month? kill switch (trigger in BS)?  Biodegradable?  Toxin/anti-toxin system to prevent HGT: Plasmid (transferrable DNA): Holin (protein that creates pores in bacterial membrane) \+  Endosylin (enzyme that degrades cell wall)  Genome (not transferable): Anti-holin (a protein that blocks holin \+ prevents lysis of the cell) In a wild type bacterium the plasmid will be transferred and not the genome so cell lysis will occur in any bacteria that receives the plasmid from our GMO by HGT. However, any bacterium that's produced from the reproduction of our GMO would obtain the plasmid and genome both so it won't undergo lysis \= self-sustaining colony   Kill switch: in desert environments, Bacillus subtilis experiences osmotic stress when water leaves the cell due to dry, high salinity conditions. The bacteria responds to the sudden environmental changes through the SigB-controlled stress response that gets activated. We could use this natural stress-sensing system to control biofilm formation so that bacteria produce a sand-binding matrix only during harsh conditions like sandstorms.  ([https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4238804/](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4238804/) \!\!)  If we plan to make a capsule or other that has to be reapplied every once in a while just before sandstorms, a previous iGEM team worked on a kill switch for Bacillus subtilis “We tailored our kill switch for Bacillus subtilis, selecting MazF as the toxin and MazE as the antitoxin, with DRE serving as our recombinase. Additionally, we carefully chose a promoter and terminator that are compatible with Bacillus subtilis, ensuring the effectiveness of our engineered system. This kill switch is shown at BBa\_K4743030.”  If we use this method, bacteria will form biofilm for a limited time before it dies. A separate mechanism will need to be used for biofilm production, however, we will have more control on environmental issues since the bacteria will self-eliminate after production of the biofilm. Another iGEM team worked on an IPTG- based proof of concept switch where bacterial survival depends on an external inducer. In the absence of IPTG, the toxin MazF is expressed and kills the cells, while in its presence, an antisense RNA blocks toxin expression and allows survival. (not adapted to environment, only labs)    Are we targeting the crust or going deeper? Crust: Wind erosion is a surface phenomenon; If wind can’t lift the thick, hardened crust, the crust can act like a shield for the loose sand underneath  \*BS produces more biofilm \+ EPS under stress (e.g. low nutrient environment) \+ stress conditions/ sporulated bacteria is good for lyophilization  [Hoor Islam](mailto:hi2151@nyu.edu)[Ndeye Astou Ndiaye](mailto:nn2705@nyu.edu)[Naya | What are the environmental/biological considerations in geotechnical engineering? \+  [Ndeye Astou Ndiaye](mailto:nn2705@nyu.edu)[Adiya Pernebay](mailto:ap9567@nyu.edu) | Tests the strength of biofilms in saturated contexts, i.e water \--\> significant in contexts of storms like now \=More water retention Homogenized biofilms improve the physical strength of sand and facilitates some of its physical properties. Even within a single desert, its environmental properties (i.e habitat and soil) differ, the targeted biofilm should be characterized by: Desert plains, rock hills or plateau Arid or semi-arid soil Subsaline, saline or hypersaline area Each hosts a diversity of species and holds different physical properties of the ecosystem. Biofilm in sand can lead to a reduction of hydraulic conductivity and can be used for groundwater containment and remediation. Biofilm has a negligible influence on the strength and stiffness of the sand but increases time-dependent creep deformation. ButNumerical simulations showed that additional creep behavior of the biofilm sand had a negligible influence on the behavior of these geotechnical engineering facilities.  Contradictory conclusions: Perkins et al. (2000)showed that Klebsiella oxytoca-produced biofilm has negligible influence on the strength and stiffness of the sand using triaxial and oedometer tests. Daniels et al. (2009) concluded thatBeijerinckia indica-produced biofilm has a decreasing effect on the soil strength of clay and clayey sand using unconfined compression tests. Banagan (2010) reported that the shear strength of Ottawa 30 sand estimated using vane shear test was increased by 15.2\~87.5% by adding biofilm-forming bacteria Flavobacterium johnsoniae.  |  | [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.bioflm.2021.100050](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.bioflm.2021.100050)  Eckardt, F.D., Maggs-Kölling, G., Marais, E., de Jager, P.C. | Difficulty of transforming B.subtilis and Why It’s Feasible for iGEM. [Sid Kong](mailto:zk2185@nyu.edu)  | *B.subtilis* strain 168\. Most genetically tractable Gram-positive bacteria. **Naturally competent**, can take up free DNA from the environment w/o electroporation or chemical treatment. This is governed by a master regulator **ComK**.  However, **lower transformation efficienc**y compared with E.coli. If higher efficiency is needed, a strain called SCK6 (derived from B.subtilis 1A751) carries an extra copy of comK under a xylose-inducible promoter.  |  | [https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3815800/](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3815800/) |

It also comes with a code-free software tool called Model Tool for reconstruction and analysis.   |  | etiBsu1209: [https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/36788025/](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/36788025/) Review of all 14 B.subtilis GEMs: [https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s10529-026-03712-w](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s10529-026-03712-w) |
| FBA for γ-PGA Pathway [Sid Kong](mailto:zk2185@nyu.edu) | Present in 168 and explicitly modeled **GECKO** enzyme-constrained model (one of the 14 models) was specifically built and experimentally **validated** for γ-PGA production optimization.  The authors identified single-reaction deletion targets that double γ-PGA concentration and production rate in engineered strains.  **What we can do**: either use the GECKO model directly or add the pgsBCA reaction to etiBsu1209 using the stoichiometries reported in the paper.  Note: while 168 carries the pgsBCA genes, **native expression is relatively low** compared to natto-derived strains (another stain). So our engineering strategy should include overexpression of pgsBCA under a **Strong** **promoter**.  Using enzymatic constraints to enable higher γ-PGA production  |  | GECKO model for γ-PGA: [https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6325765/](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6325765/) pgsBCA biochemistry review: [https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00253-002-0984-x](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00253-002-0984-x) pgsBCA cloning and expression in *B. subtilis* WB600: [https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4814633/](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4814633/) γ-PGA synthesis, gene regulation, and phylogenetics:[https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5751247/](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5751247/) Poly-L-γ-glutamic acid production without pgsA: [https://link.springer.com/article/10.1186/s13568-018-0636-x](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1186/s13568-018-0636-x) Massaiu, Ilaria et al. “Integration of enzymatic data in Bacillus subtilis genome-scale metabolic model improves phenotype predictions and enables in silico design of poly-γ-glutamic acid production strains.” Microbial cell factories vol. 18,1 3\. 9 Jan. 2019, [https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/30626384/](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/30626384/)  |
| FBA for Expolysaccharides (EPS) pathway [Sid Kong](mailto:zk2185@nyu.edu) | Genes present in 168, but **NOT in** **standard** GEMs. Because published GEMs focus on primary metabolism but this pathway belongs to the secondary/biofilm-specific metabolism.  For FBA, we would need to **manually** curate and add the EPS biosynthetic reactions to our chosen GEM Lab-adapted 168 stocks vary widely in their ability to produce biofilms, some variants are as competent as the WT strain NCIB 3610, while others are essentially biofilm-deficient. **If strong native EPS production is critical to the project**, we may need to either select a biofilm-competent 168 variant or engineer the regulatory circuitry. |  | γ-PGA contributions to biofilm (comparing NCIB 3610 and environmental isolates): [https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/microbiology/articles/10.3389/fmicb.2016.01811/full](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/microbiology/articles/10.3389/fmicb.2016.01811/full) Biofilm variation in 168 stocks: [https://www.microbiologyresearch.org/content/journal/micro/10.1099/mic.0.000371](https://www.microbiologyresearch.org/content/journal/micro/10.1099/mic.0.000371) |
| FBA for CaCO3 [Sid Kong](mailto:zk2185@nyu.edu) | Microbially induced calcite precipitation **(MICP) is not an enzymatic pathway encoded** in the B.subtilis 168 genome, meaning it has no native urease activity and no detectable intrinsic MICP capability.  What we can model is the heterologous urease pathway **added** to the GEM, the resulting ammonium and CO2 production fluxes and the metabolic cost of expressing the urease complex.  The actual calcite precipitation would be predicted by coupling these fluxes to a separate abiotic calculation of local pH, carbonate saturation and Ca2+ availability.  Biomineralization by using the mutated IcfA operon to form calcite crystals Bacillus subtilis 168 induces and influences carbonate precipitation. B. subtilis produced CaCO3 minerals by INFBM; where EPS, membrane, and cell wall structures act as nucleation sites. B. subtilis facilitates CaCO3 precipitation through non-ureolytic pathways. |  | Engineering *B. subtilis* 168 for MICP: [https://link.springer.com/article/10.1186/s12934-021-01704-1](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1186/s12934-021-01704-1) *B. subtilis* MICP with calcium salts: [https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-06688-1](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-06688-1) Barabesi, Chiara et al. “Bacillus subtilis gene cluster involved in calcium carbonate biomineralization.” Journal of bacteriology vol. 189,1 (2007): 228-35. doi:10.1128/JB.01450-06  Ferral-Pérez, et al. (2020). Novel method to achieve crystallinity of calcite by Bacillus subtilis in coupled and non-coupled calcium-carbon sources. AMB Express, 10(1). [https://doi.org/10.1186/s13568-020-01111-6](https://doi.org/10.1186/s13568-020-01111-6)  |
| What will our calcium source be in the Calcium Carbonate precipitation method? [Naya Mansour](mailto:nm5098@nyu.edu) | Desert soils may contain calcium in forms such as limestone, gypsum, or carbonate-rich dust, but much of this calcium is not readily bioavailable, as it is often locked in insoluble minerals or located below the active surface layer where erosion occurs. This means that relying on natural calcium alone would lead to inconsistent performance across sites. A more practical engineering approach is to design a system in which the calcium source is carried within the capsule itself, ensuring predictable activation after deployment. **Calcium chloride (very high risk)** remains the most straightforward and widely used option due to its high solubility and ease of dosing, but it must be used carefully, as it can trigger rapid mineral formation and lead to excessive hardening if not properly controlled.  Since we want a controlled release of this calcium, we need buffered-release capsules  **Gypsum** is particularly well suited for this purpose, as it dissolves slowly and provides a steady supply of calcium ions, reducing the risk of forming an overly rigid, concrete-like crust.  (moisture-triggered activation- needs water to slowly dissolve Gypsum and activate bacteria).  **Calcium formate** represents an especially promising alternative, as it can provide both calcium for precipitation and formate as a potential metabolic energy source for the bacteria, making it a strong candidate for an integrated system.  **Limestone**\-based approaches are also feasible, particularly when combined with a mild acid release mechanism to increase calcium availability, though they require more careful design to control dissolution rates.  Native soil calcium sources are unreliable if the composition is unknown beforehand. Calcium chloride releases Ca²⁺ too quickly and can cause excessive Calcium carbonate formation leading to hard crusts, while gypsum releases Ca²⁺ too slowly to provide effective binding within short timescales, so neither works well as a primary standalone calcium source.  **98% of calcium in desert soil carbonates comes from atmospheric input (dust \+ rain), not from the ground itself**. It needs carbonate and water to get activated  |  | 1- [https://ineris.hal.science/ineris-03137843v1/file/2021-001%20post-print.pdf](https://ineris.hal.science/ineris-03137843v1/file/2021-001%20post-print.pdf) 2- [https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s12010-014-0842-1](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s12010-014-0842-1) (Microbially induced calcium carbonate precipitation through CO2 sequestration via an engineered *Bacillus subtilis* ) 3- [https://journals.ekb.eg/article\_328400\_6be10b9e060bdf043f2211b7eeaeeb82.pdf](https://journals.ekb.eg/article_328400_6be10b9e060bdf043f2211b7eeaeeb82.pdf)  4-       [https://www.mdpi.com/2311-5637/9/3/307](https://www.mdpi.com/2311-5637/9/3/307) (limestone) 5- [https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0957582025011607](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0957582025011607) (calcium formate)  6- [https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0012821X99000904\#aep-section-id23](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0012821X99000904#aep-section-id23)  7- [https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0022024821000725](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0022024821000725)  |
| Could we find a way to use atmospheric CO2 as our carbon source? If so, how will we get the bacteria to use it? [Naya Mansour](mailto:nm5098@nyu.edu) | On the carbon side, the most realistic strategy is to use atmospheric carbon dioxide as a source for carbonate formation rather than attempting to engineer the bacteria to rely entirely on CO₂ for growth. The best approach is to enhance the natural conversion of CO₂ into carbonate ions using enzymes such as carbonic anhydrase. This allows the system to incorporate environmental CO₂ into calcium carbonate formation while still relying on a small internal energy source to maintain bacterial activity. “Recombinantly produced carbonic anhydrase successfully sequestered CO2 and converted it into calcium carbonate minerals using an engineered microbial system.”  HOWEVER this experiment worked in lab conditions with enough nutrients, calcium and moisture. We will have to check if this mechanism still holds for harsh desert conditions True autotrophy from CO₂ is much more complex because it requires both energy and reducing power. Even in engineered Escherichia coli, CO₂-based growth only worked by adding multiple pathways and supplying formate as an energy source. This means that if carbonic anhydrase alone is not sufficient, shifting to full CO₂ fixation would introduce major complexity and risk, making it unrealistic for a short-term project.   |  | 1- [https://link.springer.com/article/10.1186/s12934-024-02437-7](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1186/s12934-024-02437-7)  2- [https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0092867419312309](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0092867419312309)  |
| Abundance and Energy Sources of *Bacillus subtilis* in the Abu Dhabi Desert [Hana Elmowafy](mailto:he2161@nyu.edu) | **1\. Is B. subtilis Present in Desert Environments?** B. subtilis is genuinely ubiquitous in desert soils worldwide.  Desert-adapted strains of B. subtilis are genetically more diverse Firmicutes (the phylum containing B. subtilis) has been detected in UAE desert rhizosphere soils. B. subtilis has been isolated from desert camel feces in the region. Population density in soil is typically \~10^6 spores per gram. However, sand is the least hospitable soil type for B. subtilis population density.  **2\. Carbon and Energy Sources** Primary Carbon Sources: B. subtilis is a saprophyte  The organism degrades cellulose and hemicellulose from dead plant material.  B. subtilis can grow on very low concentrations of glucose.  Rainfall and wetting events release a pulse of carbon from the soil.  Nitrogen Sources Nitrogen is genuinely scarce in desert soils.  B. subtilis has dedicated molecular machinery to operate under nitrogen scarcity.  Bacillus species act as biological nitrogen fixers in Arabian desert soils.  Phosphorus and Micronutrients B. subtilis solubilises insoluble phosphorus from desert minerals.  B. subtilis produces siderophores to capture iron from the soil mineral matrix.   **3\. Water Availability and the Boom-Bust Growth Cycle 4\. Spore Dormancy and Long-Term Persistence 5\. Natural Precedent for Biological Soil Crusts in the Region** |  | [Learn Microbiology: Bacillus subtilis Morphology, Habitat, Diseases and Industrial Applications](https://learnmicrobiology.com/bacillus-subtilis-morphology-habitat-diseases-industrial-applications/) [Springer: Environment Shapes the Intra-species Diversity of Bacillus subtilis Isolates (2019)](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00248-019-01455-y) [Frontiers in Plant Science: High-throughput sequencing reveals the structure and metabolic resilience of desert microbiome (2024)](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/plant-science/articles/10.3389/fpls.2024.1294173/full) [Emirates Soil Museum: Soil Biodiversity of the Arabian Deserts](https://www.emiratessoilmuseum.org/digital-exhibition/section3/227) [Scientific Reports: Isolation and characterisation of a resistance Bacillus subtilis for soil stabilisation and dust alleviation (2024)](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-024-77613-1) [Environmental Microbiology: Disentangling the factors defining B. subtilis group species abundance in natural soils (2024)](https://enviromicro-journals.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/full/10.1111/1462-2920.16693) [PMC: Ecology and Genomics of Bacillus subtilis](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC2819312/) **:** [Environmental Microbiology: Disentangling B. subtilis Abundance in Natural Soils (2024) \[citing Soares et al., 2012 on hemicellulose and cellulose degradation\]](https://enviromicro-journals.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/full/10.1111/1462-2920.16693)  |
| Extracting Alginate from *P. aeruginosa [Hoor Islam](mailto:hi2151@nyu.edu)* | Sources: [Generation of a highly attenuated strain of Pseudomonas aeruginosa for commercial production of alginate \- PMC](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6922527/#mbt213411-bib-0048)  In *P. aeruginosa*, alginate is polymerized, modified and secreted by a multiprotein complex that spans the inner and outer membranes of the organism.  The proteins that make up this complex are expressed from the alginate biosynthetic operon, which contains 12 coding sequences (*algD*, *alg8*, *alg44*, *algK*, *algE*, *algG*, *algX*, *algL*, *algI*, *algJ*, *algF* and *algA*), as well as one separately localized gene (*algC*). These genes are highly conserved between bacterial species known to produce alginate.  In *P. aeruginosa*, expression of this operon is controlled by the autoregulating sigma factor AlgU, which is expressed from the alginate regulatory operon (*algU*, *mucA*, *mucB*, *mucC*, *mucD*) AlgU is a major regulator of alginate biosynthesis, and its activity is highly regulated.  A major regulator of AlgU is MucA, a protein that binds and sequesters AlgU to the inner membrane. Positive regulators of alginate biosynthesis usually act via degradation of MucA to release AlgU, leading to expression of the biosynthetic operon and alginate production.  **But P. aeruginosa is Pathogenic:** However, *P. aeruginosa* is an opportunistic pathogen that can cause life‐threatening infections in immunocompromised patients. Therefore, we sought to engineer a non‐pathogenic *P. aeruginosa* strain that is safe for commercial production of alginate. Using a homologous recombination strategy, we sequentially deleted five key pathogenicity genes from the *P. aeruginosa* chromosome, resulting in the marker‐free strain PGN5. Intraperitoneal injection of mice with PGN5 resulted in 0% mortality, while injection with wild‐type *P. aeruginosa* resulted in 95% mortality, providing evidence that the systemic virulence of PGN5 is highly attenuated.  How to combine sodium with alginate to form sodium alginate? [Pathway of sodium alginate synthesis from marine brown algae, Sargassum wightii from Sri Lanka | Discover Food | Springer Nature Link](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44187-021-00001-5) This paper shows how to combine sodium with alginate obtained from marine brown algae. We could use the same approach for alginate obtained from *P. aeruginosa* but then why not just use brown algae to make sodium alginate then? ![][image1] Figure form [5\. ALGINATE](https://www.fao.org/4/y4765e/y4765e08.htm) on how to produce sodium alginate from seaweed  The purified alginate is typically treated with sodium-based solutions or processed during the final purification stages to ensure the carboxyl groups are neutralized as sodium salts (sodium alginate). This form is preferred for its high solubility in water and its ability to cross-link with divalent cations (like calcium) to form stable gels. How is alginate form *P. aeruginosa different* from alginate from brown algae? The bacterial alginate’s high stickiness (due to acetylation) might be beneficial for initial sand grain binding. However, the lower structural rigidity may mean it doesn't hold the sand shape as firmly as commercial seaweed-derived sodium alginate.   |  |  |
| B. subtilis strain selection [Sid Kong](mailto:zk2185@nyu.edu) | Selection Criteria: ease of transformation; toolkit availability; FBA compatibility; Biofilm capability First instinct: DK1042, biofilm-proficient *and* naturally transformable — NCIB 3610 with a *comI* Q12L mutation that restores competence Concern: transformability, 100x lower compared with 168 (1A1) Decision: order 1A1 and DK1042 1A1 (168 trypC2) Reference strain for FBA models Native host for most toolkits Highly transformable trpC2: needs 50mg/L tryptophan in minimal media for growth ❌ Biofilm-defective DK1042 (3A38) Robust biofilm \+ native EPS (essential for MICP) \~99.9% same metabolic genome as 168 \-\> FBA still works ❌ \~100× lower transformability ![][image2] Top priority is to get a proof of concept to show our γ-PGA  \+ second approach works. Once validated, moving it into a biofilm strain is just a transformation step. |  | Strain selection: [https://journals.asm.org/doi/10.1128/jb.00696-13](https://journals.asm.org/doi/10.1128/jb.00696-13) [https://journals.asm.org/doi/10.1128/genomea.00364-17](https://journals.asm.org/doi/10.1128/genomea.00364-17) [https://journals.asm.org/doi/10.1128/jb.01542-10](https://journals.asm.org/doi/10.1128/jb.01542-10) FBA / γ-PGA modeling: [https://link.springer.com/article/10.1186/s12934-018-1052-2](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1186/s12934-018-1052-2) γ-PGA engineering precedent: [https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1155/2016/3073949](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1155/2016/3073949) MICP:  [https://link.springer.com/article/10.1186/s12934-021-01704-1](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1186/s12934-021-01704-1) |
| Engineer EPS, γ-PGA, or both? [Sid Kong](mailto:zk2185@nyu.edu) | EPS is the primary glue, also essential for calcite nucleation for MICP Defective in 1A1; Robust in DK1042 γ-PGA is accessory \- water binding, grain aggregation, drought tolerance Silent in 168; low in DK1042 Plan γ-PGA: replace weak native the promoter with a stronger one \+ clone \+  anything we learned from FBA later on.  EPS: do nothing about it. Operon too complicated & not FBA-ready Summary: Order 1A1 and DK1042. Engineer γ-PGA only. Native EPS from DK1042 covers biofilm and MICP. |  |  |
| Extraction of sodium alginate [Hoor Islam](mailto:hi2151@nyu.edu) | **From Pseudomonas 1\. Background:** *P. aeruginosa* is a ubiquitous Gram‐negative bacterium found in soil, water and man‐made environments.  An opportunistic pathogen that can cause life‐threatening infections in immunocompromised patients (caused by 5 genes: *toxA*, *plcH*, *phzM*, *wapR* and *aroA*) *Pseudomonas aeruginosa* is also capable of forming biofilms that protect the organism from environmental stressors, including dehydration, predation, antibiotics and host defences   *P. aeruginosa* biofilm is rich in alginate  **2\. Why is this method better than brown seaweed?** Currently, all alginate used commercially is extracted from brown seaweed (Peteiro, [2018](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6922527/#mbt213411-bib-0045)). However, increases in ocean temperature and CO levels as a result of climate change are a looming threat to seaweed habitats and alginate yields. Additionally, the ability to tailor the composition of seaweed alginate (e.g. M:G ratio) to suit specific applications is limited. Thus, there is a growing need for more reliable and customizable sources of alginate. The process of harvesting and extracting alginate from seaweed is labour-intensive, requiring harsh acid/base treatments and yielding large amounts of contaminated wastewater. In contrast, bacterial alginate can be extracted from the culture medium via ethanol precipitation, a procedure that does not require treatment with harsh chemicals.   **3\. Production of alginate containing biofilm (pathway)** In *P. aeruginosa*, alginate is polymerized, modified and secreted by a multiprotein complex that spans the inner and outer membranes of the organism   The proteins that make up this complex are expressed from the alginate biosynthetic operon, which contains 12 coding sequences , as well as one separately localized gene In *P. aeruginosa*, expression of this operon is controlled by the autoregulating sigma factor AlgU, which is expressed from the alginate regulatory operon  A major regulator of AlgU is MucA, a protein that binds and sequesters AlgU to the inner membrane  Positive regulators of alginate biosynthesis usually act via degradation of MucA to release AlgU, leading to expression of the biosynthetic operon and alginate production.   **4\. Possible Limitations of Extraction \+ Solutions:** Pathogenicity Solution 1: Using a homologous recombination strategy, sequentially delete five key pathogenicity genes from the *P. aeruginosa* chromosome, resulting in the marker‐free strain PGN5 (they did this here: [Generation of a highly attenuated strain of Pseudomonas aeruginosa for commercial production of alginate \- PMC](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6922527/)) . Because five pathogenicity gene sequences were completely deleted from different parts of the PGN5 chromosome, it is unlikely for this strain to revert to its original level of virulence. PGN5 is also auxotrophic for aromatic amino acids and is unlikely to survive in case of accidental release into the environment. PAO1 wild strain/ pathogenic → PGN5 strain with deleted genes/ not pathogenic Solution 2: Buy pathogen-free strain  Low and inconsistent alginate yields in wild‐type strains  Solution 1: Overexpression of a novel activator of alginate biosynthesis, MucE, induces a mucoid phenotype and consistent overproduction of alginate by activating the protease AlgW to cleave MucA, thus activating AlgU (they did this here: [Regulated proteolysis controls mucoid conversion in Pseudomonas aeruginosa \- PMC](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC1876579/) ) **5\. Extraction of alginate \+ making sodium alginate:** 2 stages ([Generation of a highly attenuated strain of Pseudomonas aeruginosa for commercial production of alginate \- PMC](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6922527/)) The *P. aeruginosa* strains were cultured for 72 h. Alginic acid was then precipitated by adding three volumes of ethanol. The precipitate was vacuum-filtered through a Twill Dutch weave wire cloth (5 μm mesh) and dried in a vacuum oven  To produce sodium alginate from the dried alginic acid, the authors dissolved it in NaOH adjusted to pH 8.5–9. This formed sodium alginate, which was then re-precipitated with ethanol, filtered again, and dried in a vacuum oven  Should we get alginate from seaweed and show that the production of alginate from pseudoman is the same or higher that alginate from seaweed/ we could just incorporate results from this study which compares the two: [Generation of a highly attenuated strain of Pseudomonas aeruginosa for commercial production of alginate \- PMC](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6922527/)  **Brown seaweed Background** All brown algae contain alginic acid (alginate) in their cell walls which can be easily extracted Currently the sole source of commercially available alginate   **Why is this method better than Pseudomans?** Extraction is extremely simple High yield  Safe  **Limitations:** NOT conserving the environment (damage marine ecosystem \+ fish habitats) Also event thought the source is natural, the process still uses harsh chemical/acids Inconsistent M/G ratio (Mannuronic acid and Guluronic acid ratio) Less  control over structure. With bacteria we can ‘design’ it so that polymer has a certain length/ composition/ arrangement but with seaweed we are using what’s naturally made so we can't really control how the length/ composition turns out to be   **Extraction of Sodium Alginate (extremelyyy simple process reported here [\*IJRTI](https://ijrti.org/papers/IJRTI2112001.pdf)  :) )** Dry the sea weed for one week and chop into small pieces In wet chopped sea weed add formalin (formaldehyde \+methanol)  Add sodium carbonate solution.  Centrifuge process: centrifuge at 3000rpm at 15 minutes To separate the liquid to solid (at the end this phase, the seaweed has been separated in to an alginate) The sea weed residue(slurry) is separated from sodium alginate (liquid containing alginate) and the slurry is discarded.  Add HCl to the sodium alginate solution, it forms as ALGINIC ACID GEL  Water is evaporated using dewatering process(filtration)  Sodium carbonate is added  The mixture is heated to 50 to 98oC and completely evaporated  And it forms sodium Alginate Sodium Alginate is crystallized to form Alginate  |  | [Generation of a highly attenuated strain of Pseudomonas aeruginosa for commercial production of alginate \- PMC](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6922527/) [**\*IJRTI**](https://ijrti.org/papers/IJRTI2112001.pdf)  [Regulated proteolysis controls mucoid conversion in Pseudomonas aeruginosa \- PMC](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC1876579/) |
| Prong 2: anchoring CA on the cell surface | **why are we trying to anchor CA on the surface instead of just secreting it into the biofilm?** In prong two, the bacteria’s job is to convert CO}\_2$ into bicarbonate, which then transitions into carbonate to react with calcium ions. The bacterial cell wall naturally acts as the nucleation site where this calcium carbonate precipitation happens because its negative charge attracts and concentrates the positive calcium ions nearby. Since the cell wall is the exact physical location where the crystal formation occurs, the carbonic anhydrase needs to be anchored directly to that same spot. Secreting the enzyme into the open extracellular polymeric substance (EPS) would decouple the reaction, leaving you just "hoping" the generated bicarbonate randomly finds the calcium ions in the surrounding environment. |  |  |
| Non-ureolytic pathway (without CA)  | This paper highlights the reason that the ureolytic pathway is undesirable and includes disadvantages of many other methods of sand stabilization (including biopolymers). This paper is useful for the explained ureolytic pathways \+ the disadvantages mentioned above. The non-ureolytic pathway is different to what we are considering though. Here is an AI generated summary of the paper: This paper investigates a new non-ureolytic microbial induced carbonate precipitation (MICP) method for stabilizing sand dunes and reducing wind erosion without producing harmful ammonia, a common by-product of traditional ureolytic MICP. The researchers used two bacteria, Bacillus subtilis and Bacillus amyloliquefaciens, together with two calcium sources, calcium formate and calcium acetate, to precipitate calcium carbonate (CaCO₃) that binds sand grains together. They found that calcium formate-based systems, especially the combination of calcium formate with Bacillus subtilis (FS), produced the highest amount of CaCO₃ and gave the best resistance to wind erosion and sand bombardment. The precipitated CaCO₃ mainly formed as vaterite crystals coating and cementing sand particles. Wind tunnel experiments showed that treated dunes gained much higher surface strength and resisted wind speeds above 25 m/s at sufficient application rates. Importantly, the study demonstrated that non-ureolytic MICP can achieve performance comparable to conventional ureolytic methods while avoiding ammonia release, making it a more environmentally friendly option for desert soil stabilization and dust suppression. |  | [New non-ureolytic heterotrophic microbial induced carbonate precipitation for suppression of sand dune wind erosion \- PMC](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10086056/) (recommended by Ellen Piercy) |
| CA displayed on the cell surface on E.coli used to capture carbon and form calcium carbonate | This paper discusses the way Carbonic anhydrase (CA) is able to use atmospheric CO2 to create carbonate, then calcium carbonate, when being displayed on the surface of the cell. It is exactly what we want to do but using a different chassis (B.subtilis not E.coli), so a different fusion protein. P.S ‘ping-pong mechanism’ \= double displacement reaction w enzyme.  This paper developed an engineered *E. coli* system that captures CO₂ and converts it into calcium carbonate (CaCO₃) using surface-displayed carbonic anhydrase (CA). The researchers attached highly active CA enzymes, especially SazCA from *Sulfurihydrogenibium azorense*, onto the outer membrane of *E. coli* cells using an ice nucleation protein anchor. This allowed CO₂ to react directly at the cell surface, avoiding membrane transport limitations and significantly improving catalytic efficiency. The enzyme rapidly converted dissolved CO₂ into bicarbonate, which then reacted with externally supplied calcium ions to precipitate CaCO₃ under alkaline conditions. The engineered surface-display strain produced much more CaCO₃ than intracellular-expression strains and showed greater thermal, pH, and long-term stability than free enzyme systems. Importantly, the process achieved CO₂ mineralization without using urea, meaning it avoided ammonia production associated with traditional ureolytic MICP. The study demonstrates a promising environmentally friendly strategy for CO₂ sequestration and biomineralization using reusable whole-cell biocatalysts.  |  | [Surface display of carbonic anhydrase on Escherichia coli for CO2 capture and mineralization \- ScienceDirect](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405805X21000764) [The "Ping-Pong" Mechanism \- Chemistry LibreTexts](https://chem.libretexts.org/Bookshelves/Biological_Chemistry/Supplemental_Modules_\(Biological_Chemistry\)/Enzymes/Enzymatic_Kinetics/Ping-pong_mechanisms) \[ping-pong\] |
| Overview of cell surface and spore surface display technologies  | This paper is like an introduction to surface display technology for Bacillus subtilis. It gives a few options that we might want to look into/eliminate.  “However, this approach remains temporary and demands carefully regulated conditions to maintain bacterial viability” look into this statement The paper reviews how *Bacillus subtilis* surface display technology uses bacterial cells or spores to display proteins, enzymes, and antigens on their surface for biotechnology applications. Because *B. subtilis* is safe, easy to engineer, and forms highly resistant spores, it is useful in industrial bioprocessing, environmental cleanup, vaccine development, and drug delivery. The review highlights applications such as enzyme immobilization for stable biocatalysis, pollutant degradation and heavy metal removal, biosensors for contaminant detection, and oral or nasal vaccines against viruses and bacteria. It also discusses spore-based therapeutic delivery systems for drugs and cancer treatments. Overall, the authors conclude that combining the durability of spores with advances in genetic engineering makes *B. subtilis* a promising platform for sustainable biomanufacturing, healthcare, and environmental biotechnology, although challenges like improving protein expression and stability still remain. |  | [Bacillus subtilis surface display technology: applications in bioprocessing and sustainable manufacturing \- PMC](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11909954/)  |
| Transformation of Bacillus subtilis with CA (CA genes from Thermovibrio ammonificans, B.megaterium, B. subtilis, and S. pasteurii) | This paper is useful because it has transformed our chassis with CA (though not as a fusion protein to be displayed on the surface) This paper compared the CA from T. ammonificans, B. megaterium, B. subtilis, and S. pasteurii. Both the B.megaterium ones are beta CAs. One CA gene from B.megaterium is determined to be the best, meanwhile the B.subtilis and S.pasteurii don’t do much. A lot of experiments and conclusions were drawn using Cell Free Extracts (CFE), which doesn’t factor the polymerization of the CA.  However, B.megaterium is not a thermophile. Their experiment was conducted at 30 degrees.  “Restriction endonucleases and DNA-modifying enzymes were obtained from ThermoFisher Scientific (UK). All oligonucleotides were obtained from Integrated DNA Technologies (USA). Plasmid pHT253 was obtained from MoBiTec (DE). All other reagents were obtained from Fisher (UK) unless otherwise stated. “ for engineering CA into B.subtilis  For paper \[1\] transformation lab work:  \- order CA genes  Insert genes into plasmid **pHT253** (Pgrac100 promoter drives gene expression in B.subtilis) using restriction enzymes **BamHI** and **XhoI E. coli TOP10** for plasmid propagation  After plasmid verification insert plasmid into **Bacillus subtilis strain 168** Control systems: **Empty vector pHT253** in B. subtilis as a negative control, **Sporosarcina pasteurii DSM33** as a positive/control model for MICP, **Bacillus megaterium DSM32** to simulate natural urease and CA expression.   | ***Thermovibrio ammonificans*** (thermophilic bacteria, tetramer), ***Bacillus megaterium*** (stops sporulation/protein formation/growth at 40-50, dimer or tetramer), and ***Sulfurihydrogenibium yellowstonense*** (thermopholic bacteria, thermostable proteins, dimer), ***Sulfurihydrogenibium azorense*** (thermophilic bacteria, thermostable proteins, dimer) Link \[2\] has more information on B.megaterium.  Link \[3\] claims that “SazCA is the most active CA known to date” 1- TaCA is the best. Even though it does not have the best mineral yield it is proven to withstand desert conditions.  Link \[4\] proves TaCA can withstand the harshness of the desert and.  | \[1\] [Microbially induced calcium carbonate precipitation through CO 2 sequestration via an engineered Bacillus subtilis](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1186/s12934-024-02437-7) \[2\] [https://www.microbiologyresearch.org/content/journal/micro/10.1099/00221287-137-4-787](https://www.microbiologyresearch.org/content/journal/micro/10.1099/00221287-137-4-787) \[3\] [https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0968089612007687?via%3Dihub\#b0085](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0968089612007687?via%3Dihub#b0085) \[4\] [https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212982019306973](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212982019306973)   |
| An Overview of the Bacterial Carbonic Anhydrases | This paper explains the different types (7) of CA and bacterial CA (3).  Insight into the ways they work, including being active as a monomer, dimer, tetramer etc |  | [https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5746736/](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5746736/)  |
| Producing a High-Density Surface Mineralization Module: CWBMLytE anchor system | This paper transforms Bacillus subtilis, development of a high-density protein surface display system utilizing the cell wall-binding module of the LytE protein CWBMLytE, consisting of three N-terminal LysM motifs from a Bacillus subtilis cell wall hydrolase . These constructs are primarily based on the pWB980 expression vector, which uses the P43 promoter for transcription and the SacB signal sequence to direct proteins to the cell surface. The primary constructs used in the study are categorized into control modules and two experimental fusion series designed to evaluate the impacts of structural orientation and linker length on protein display. The control group includes CWBMLytE, which produces a standalone cell wall-binding module (CWBM) derived from the LytE protein consisting of three tandem LysM motifs, and CWBMLytB, a comparative construct utilizing three type II cell wall-binding domains instead. A third control, Bla, produces only the mature form of the TEM-beta-lactamase reporter protein without an anchor to serve as a secreted control. The experimental BE series features an N-terminal reporter setup where the beta-lactamase reporter is fused to the N-terminal end of the CWBMLytE anchor to test how varying linker lengths affect enzymatic activity and display density. This series includes B5E, B32E, and B55E, which contain linkers of 5, 32, and 55 amino acids, respectively, as well as B123E, which features a 123-amino-acid linker specifically designed to investigate if an extended sequence is necessary to project proteins through the cell wall. Conversely, the EB series explores a C-terminal reporter configuration to determine if positioning the anchor at the N-terminal end—its natural orientation in the native LytE protein—improves performance; this is represented by the E48B construct, which separates the N-terminal CWBMLytE anchor and the C-terminal beta-lactamase reporter with a 48-amino-acid linker. **Key Design Features: Linker Composition:** All linkers used in these fusions are rich in **glycine, proline, and serine** to ensure they are flexible, disordered, and hydrophilic. **Optimal Performance:** The **E48B** construct was found to be the most effective, as it showed higher cell wall-binding affinity and less formation of inclusion bodies compared to the BE series. **Notable points:**  **High-Density Display:** This paper describes a B. subtilis system capable of displaying 1.2 × 10⁷ molecules per cell. This high density is ideal for maximizing the "ping-pong mechanism" (double displacement) of enzymes like CA at the cell surface. **Surface Accessibility:** The paper confirms that proteins displayed via the CWBMLytE anchor are distributed across the "entire cell surface" and are fully accessible to external molecules. This would allow CO₂ to react directly at the cell surface, as desired in your E. coli summary, avoiding the need for the gas to penetrate deep into the cell. **Protease-Deficient Strain (WB800):** This paper uses WB800 strain, which is deficient in eight extracellular and cell wall-bound proteases, specifically to prevent the degradation of surface-displayed proteins. **Non-Covalent Equilibrium:** The system produces excess fusion proteins that exist in equilibrium between the culture medium and the cell wall. This ensures that even if some surface proteins are lost, the cell wall remains saturated with functional enzymes as long as the bacteria are active. |  | [https://enviromicro-journals.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/epdf/10.1111/j.1751-7915.2007.00017.x?saml\_referrer=](https://enviromicro-journals.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/epdf/10.1111/j.1751-7915.2007.00017.x?saml_referrer=) |
| Sortase Technology vs Fusion Protein  | The question shouldn’t be comparing these two because they are the same:  ![][image3] The sequence would be composed of:  N-terminal Signal Peptide::To ensure the carbonic anhydrase is secreted out of the B. subtilis cell.  Carbonic Anhydrase Gene: The sequence responsible for catalyzing the hydration of CO2.  Sorting Signal (LPDTS Motif): You must include the Leucine-Proline-Aspartate-Threonine-Serine motif at the C-terminus so the sortase can recognize it.  Cell Wall-Binding Motif (CWM): A domain that facilitates the initial interaction with the cell wall before the covalent bond is formed that can be: C-terminal : YhcS sortase enzyme or YhcR ![][image4] A customized picture based on \[1\] |  | \[1\][https://link.springer.com/article/10.1186/s12934-021-01701-4](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1186/s12934-021-01701-4) \[2\][https://journals.asm.org/doi/10.1128/spectrum.01121-25](https://journals.asm.org/doi/10.1128/spectrum.01121-25)  |
| Linker Research  |  |  | \[1\] [https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3726540/](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3726540/)  |
| **There is a problem with B. subtilis strain choice** | The previous papers use WB800, where all 8 proteases are knocked out, so they don't degrade the enzymes. But in the strains we want, the proteases are active.  If we use a standard lab strain like **1A1 (168)** or a wild biofilm strain like **DK1042**, these active proteases will treat our engineered surface enzymes as a food source and rapidly degrade them, destroying our system's MICP activity. While the protease-deficient **WB800** strain guarantees maximum protein display density by eliminating these destructive enzymes, it lacks the rugged biofilm-forming capabilities that a strain like DK1042 naturally possesses to robustly cement sand particles together. Technically, **yes, WB800 can still form a biofilm, but it is incredibly weak, thin, and fragile** compared to a wild strain like DK1042. WB800 retains the baseline genetic machinery to stick to a surface, but it cannot produce the robust, thick, structural matrix necessary to withstand wind tunnel erosion or mechanically bridge the gaps between shifting grains of desert sand. It remains strictly a tool for your initial, high-yield liquid lab assays. Options we have: Switch to WB800 purely for proof of concept and tested transformation methods, but compromise the realism, as this specific strain won’t produce enough biofilm to be actually used. Stick to DK1042, but risk our enzymes getting degraded.  **The Overproduction Gamble:** Because the P43 promoter on your pWB980 plasmid is incredibly strong, it might pump out the fusion protein faster than the native proteases of 1A1 or DK1042 can degrade it. You can run the experiment to see if the "non-covalent equilibrium" allows the cell wall to stay saturated despite active degradation. **CRISPR the Strains:** If you love DK1042's sand-binding properties but hate its proteases, your team’s future milestone could be using CRISPR to knock out the wprA (wall-bound protease) gene directly inside the DK1042 chromosome. This gives you a custom "DK1042-MICP" strain that has both robust biofilms and stable surface proteins. I dont think we have time for CRISPR tho…  |  | [https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC126797/](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC126797/)  |
| Displaying Carbonic Anhydrase on the surface of Bacillus subtilis (Way 1):  This is what they say the used: [https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC126797/](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC126797/)  The paper notes that your insert must be cloned immediately downstream of the SacB signal peptide. Ensure the code aligns perfectly so that the ribosome reads SacB \-\> CWBMLytE Anchor \-\> L48 Linker \-\> CA Enzyme as one continuous message. N-terminal CWBMLytE anchor and the C-terminal Carbonic Anhydrase with a 48-amino-acid linker (L48) Locate the complete sequence for plasmid pWB980, isolating the P43 promoter and SacB signal sequence.  | ![][image5] Vector used in CWBMLytE anchor system paper. We are utilizing the **SacB signal peptide sequence** derived from the *B. subtilis sacB* gene as an N-terminal translocation tag. This specific sequence optimizes the secretion of our target Carbonic Anhydrase fusion protein across the cytoplasmic membrane, allowing the CWBMLytE domain to anchor effectively into the cell wall. **Pros and cons of this approach (under review of sources, but Gemini did provide them): ** ![][image6] | The text constructed pWB980-LytE-L48-Bla for production of E48B by hand, but if we will order it it doesn't matter. **Still need to identify the sequences for LytE and L48. The paper produces the linked from two halves of big primers, but we should have a better way. We can in theory extract the sequence for L48 from the given primers, but I am too lazy for that now. (Adiya)   [Naya Mansour](mailto:nm5098@nyu.edu)** (WB800 / WB800HM those strains were found ideal if we see that CA needs help folding/ or is being disrupted by protease wrpa on the cell wall.**)  for our project we should consider LytE anchor → linker → CA because according to the papers, putting the anchor before makes CA able to have higher cell wall binding affinity.  How to make sure the anchoring works: ** “Surface exposure and accessibility of these proteins were confirmed by **immunofluorescence microscopy**.” in case the CA won’t be able to be distinguished by the glow (a concern brought up by the Pi’s), a paper suggested choosing an antibody big enough to not be able to go inside the cell wall, so it has to react with the protein we engineer. We use this immunofluorescence microscopy on the antibody because if we are able to see it, it necessarily binds to the cell wall. Procedure of paper: They tested three bacterial groups: **Control bacteria** with empty plasmid. Bacteria producing **LytE anchor alone**. Bacteria producing LytE anchor \+ 48 amino acid linker \+ β-lactamase. stains: DNA stain  Hydrophobic stain for membrane/surface-like regions. Antibody stain \= shows where LytE or β-lactamase is. (fluorescent antibodies)  **Confocal microscopy** confirms cell-wall localization. **LiCL elution** (elution by a strong salt) **Biochemical fractionation:** generated protoplasts with lysozyme, separated the wall associated material from the protoplast fraction and used french press or sonication to release intracellular material. (bad result in paper, it showed that only mature fusion was able to the displayed on cell wall.  **Western Blot Scatchard analysis**   | [https://enviromicro-journals.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/epdf/10.1111/j.1751-7915.2007.00017.x?saml\_referrer=](https://enviromicro-journals.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/epdf/10.1111/j.1751-7915.2007.00017.x?saml_referrer=) [https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC126797/](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC126797/) pWB980 plasmid:  |
| In fusion protein design, choosing the outer membrane protein | The outer membrane protein (OMP) you choose should possess its own N-terminal signal peptide for translocation through one of the below pathways. For context, ![][image7] The OMP gets translated in the cytoplasm, and so for it to be inserted into the outer membrane, it needs to be translocated across the inner membrane. OMPs like OmpA or an autotransporter like AIDA-I get translocated through the Sec pathway. Proteins can also be stably localized to the bacterial surface through lipoprotein anchoring, wherein lipid-modified signal peptides direct covalent attachment of proteins to the inner leaflet of the OM |  | [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.addr.2025.115701](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.addr.2025.115701) |
| Co-expressing with the sortase enzyme in B. subtilis WASD (ΔwprA ΔsigD) cells stable and site-specific anchoring of CALB directly on the bacterial surface (Thr) \- NH \-    Covalent Amide Bond  | **Primers:  Forward Primer:** Include a **XhoI** restriction site at the 5' end. **Reverse Primer:** Include a **BamHI** restriction site at the 3' end (ensuring you remove the CA stop codon so translation continues into the C-terminal cell wall sorting signal). In the original study, they have previously expressed CALB (targeted gene) and A in another plasmid, then used RE to take the genes out of the last and insert CALB it here. If we decide to go with this method, we can trim these steps. We just have to add B. subtilis sortase (YhcS) into the plasmid and substitute CALB to CA.   E. coli cells were cultured at 37°C in Luria-Bertani (LB) medium, which consisted of 1% (wt/vol) tryptone, 0.5% (wt/vol) yeast extract, and 1% (wt/vol) NaCl. The medium was supplemented with 50 µg/mL ampicillin for antibiotic selection. For protein production, B. subtilis cells were grown in super-rich medium (SRM) containing Bacto tryptose (25 g/L), yeast extract (20 g/L), dipotassium phosphate (3 g/L), and glucose (4.5 g/L) at pH 7.5. Cultures were supplemented with 25 µg/mL tetracycline and incubated at 30°C for 14 hours before harvesting for downstream analyses. **The E. coli advantage:** *E. coli* has incredibly high transformation efficiency. It will readily take up those fragile, freshly ligated plasmids and replicate them into billions of exact copies overnight. **Steps in the Sortase-Mediated Surface Display Method (in the study)**   **Gene Amplification:** The *CA* gene is amplified via PCR using specific primers (LIPF1 and LIPR1) that introduce *XhoI* and *BamHI* restriction sites. **Plasmid Construction (Digestion and Ligation):** The existing SpA gene is excised from the pSpA-YhcS plasmid using *XhoI* and *BamHI* enzymes, and the digested CALB fragment is ligated into the remaining backbone to create the recombinant plasmid **pCALB-YhcS**. **Cloning and Screening in *E. coli*:** Competent *E. coli* DH5α cells are transformed with the new plasmid via the thermal shock (calcium chloride) method for screening and DNA sequencing verification. **Transformation of *B. subtilis*:** The verified pCALB-YhcS plasmid is introduced into competent *Bacillus subtilis* WASD cells using Spizizen’s natural transformation approach. **Culturing and Induction:** The engineered *B. subtilis* cells are grown in a super-rich medium (SRM) containing tetracycline. Expression of the CALB gene and the *yhcS* sortase gene is driven constitutively by the tetracycline promoter (*tetR*). **Harvesting and Verification:** Cells are harvested by centrifugation. The cell wall fraction is isolated via lysozyme treatment ("enzymatic shaving") and analyzed using SDS-PAGE to confirm that the 33 kDa CALB protein is successfully displayed on the exterior surface. **Other Details:** **Recombinant Expression Plasmid:** **pCALB-YhcS Shuttle Vector Backbone:** This plasmid is built on the **pHY300** / **pHY300PLK** backbone. It is a shuttle vector compatible with both *E. coli* (for easy gene manipulation/cloning) and *Bacillus subtilis* (for expression), and it contains ampicillin and tetracycline resistance markers. **Covalent Bonding:** The system relies on **covalent attachment** to permanently anchor the enzyme to the cell. The native *B. subtilis* sortase enzyme (YhcS) cleaves a specific C-terminal cell wall sorting signal recognition motif (**LPDTS**) on the modified CALB enzyme and chemically binds it directly to the peptidoglycan layer of the bacterial cell wall. This strong covalent link prevents enzyme leaching during reuse cycles. |  | [https://journals.asm.org/doi/10.1128/spectrum.01121-25](https://journals.asm.org/doi/10.1128/spectrum.01121-25)  |
| YuaB (BslA) Platform | **Information on YuaB (non cov):**  The protein BslA (officially formerly known as YuaB) is a surface hydrophobic protein that plays a major role in biofilm formation and serves as an efficient anchoring motif for cell surface display systems in Bacillus subtilis \[1, 3\]. To function as an anchoring mechanism, BslA relies on a 28-amino-acid signal peptide at its N-terminus—a component of the Sec transport system—which is absolutely vital for its secretion and function; removing this sequence (N-terminal) completely abolishes wild-type functionality and prevents the protein from being detected in the cytoplasm \[1, 2\]. “Flow cytometry of recombinant strain and its protoplast using FITC-Anti His6 antibody, verified that YuaB locate in plasma membrane and protrude to the outside of cell wall, which means YuaB can be used as very efficient anchoring motif”\[1\]. In surface display applications, target proteins or tags, such as a His6 tag \[1\] or β-CGTase \[3,4\], are fused to the C-terminus of BslA \[1, 3\]. Production of this fusion protein can be further stimulated by fusing a p43 promoter (which is transcribed by B. subtilis σ55 and σ37) to its N-terminus \[1\]. Regarding its method of localization, early unbiased secretome studies tentatively presumed BslA to be an extracellular protein \[1, 2\]. However, Western blot analysis of fractionated biofilms clearly indicates that BslA is actually a cell-associated protein found within cell lysates rather than the biofilm matrix or growth medium \[2\], where it is noncovalently bound to the cell wall \[2\]. While independent evidence from immunogold labeling electron microscopy confirms that BslA is associated with the cell wall \[5\], a localization controversy exists because confocal laser scanning microscopy shows exogenously labeled BslA covering the outer layer of complex colonies, suggesting it localizes to the matrix fraction of pellicles \[5\]. This experimental discrepancy likely arises from differences in cell and matrix fractionation techniques, specifically the use of mild sonication versus boiling in SDS sample buffer \[5\]. Ultimately, these independent observations point to a possible dual localization mechanism where a portion of newly synthesized BslA initially localizes to the cell wall before being pushed to the surface of the biofilm \[5\]. This unique dual-distribution property allows it to simultaneously support biofilm structure and anchor displayed enzymes, providing a robust, highly stable platform for continuous industrial biocatalysis \[3\]. “BslA was found within the intercellular spaces and associated with the outer surface of the cells ([Fig. 2B](https://www.pnas.org/doi/10.1073/pnas.1306390110#fig02)). Typically 25% of the surface area at the base of the pellicle was composed of BslA ([Fig. S1B](https://www.pnas.org/lookup/doi/10.1073/pnas.1306390110#supplementary-materials)).” \[6\]  Direct quotes provided here: [https://docs.google.com/document/d/1HtSpY2t3QFpbrdg9zBk3Ih8AZXPU-kThYT4X3BeBjao/edit?usp=sharing](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1HtSpY2t3QFpbrdg9zBk3Ih8AZXPU-kThYT4X3BeBjao/edit?usp=sharing) . **Method used in one of the studies:** The study aimed to develop a cell surface display system in *Bacillus subtilis* by using the biofilm component YuaB as an anchoring motif. This system was designed to bypass the costly and complex purification steps associated with traditional spore-based display systems. **Cell Strains Expression Host:** *Bacillus subtilis* DB104, a two-protease deficient strain (*trpC2 nprE aprE*), was used as the host for displaying the YuaB-His₆ tag. **Cloning Host:** *Escherichia coli* DH5α was used for genetic manipulation and plasmid transformation. **Important Plasmids and Vectors pSDJH100:** An *E. coli*/*B. subtilis* shuttle vector (based on pCSK1) used as the starting backbone. **pDE100:** An intermediate vector created by inserting the strong, overlapping p43 promoter into the pSDJH100 backbone to maximize surface expression. **pDE100-Y1:** The final recombinant expression vector, constructed by inserting the *yuaB* gene and a C-terminal His₆ tag into pDE100. **Key Methodological Steps Plasmid Construction:** The p43 promoter was amplified using PCR and ligated into the pSDJH100 vector using BamHI and PstI restriction enzymes. The *yuaB* gene was then amplified from the *B. subtilis* DB104 chromosome using a reverse primer that integrated a His₆ tag sequence. This fragment was inserted into pDE100 using PstI and XhoI restriction enzymes to yield pDE100-Y1. **Protoplast Isolation:** To confirm whether YuaB localizes in the plasma membrane, researchers isolated protoplasts by removing the bacterial peptidoglycan layer. *B. subtilis* cells were harvested, washed in a sucrose-based SET buffer, and incubated with lysozyme and phenylmethane sulfonyl fluoride (PMSF) at 37°C for 1 hour to strip the cell walls. **Flow Cytometry Verification:** To verify surface expression, both whole vegetative cells and protoplasts of the recombinant strain were reacted with an FITC-labeled anti-His₆ tag antibody on ice for 1 hour. The samples were then analyzed using a flow cytometer to detect fluorescence, confirming the successful expression and protrusion of the YuaB-His₆ tag on the cell surface. **Heavy Metal Adsorption Application:** To test the functional utility of the displayed His₆ tag, the recombinant bacteria were incubated in solutions containing divalent heavy metals, specifically Cu²⁺ and Ni²⁺, for 1 hour at 37°C. After centrifugation, the remaining metal ions in the supernatant were measured using atomic absorption spectroscopy to calculate the adsorption capacity of the engineered cells. **The Evidence:** The BslA-overexpressing strain (+bslA) produced **2.8 times** (280%) the attached biofilm biomass compared to the wild-type strain. Visually, the overexpressing strain formed highly robust, wrinkled, and elastic folds in the medium that remained structurally intact even after the culture medium was removed, whereas the bslA-deleted strain (Delta bslA) formed a thin, loose, and cracked biofilm. \[1\] | Vectors used in previous studies discussed here do not align with vectors Sid found for prompt 1\.  | [https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s12257-020-0397-7](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s12257-020-0397-7) (Uses pSDJH100: An E. coli/B. subtilis shuttle vector (based on pCSK1) [https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3165672/](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3165672/)  [https://link.springer.com/article/10.1186/s13068-025-02635-4](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1186/s13068-025-02635-4) [https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-024-81490-z](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-024-81490-z) (pBE2R plasmid was used as the vector for the display of anchor protein BslA and β-cyclodextrin glycosyltransferase)  they used “an optimized linker with the following sequence: AAAGAATCTGGCTCTGTTTCTTCTGAACAACTTGCTCAATTCCGTTCTCTTGAT.”  [https://www.rug.nl/research/gelifes/tres/\_pdf/ko\_eamm12.pdf](https://www.rug.nl/research/gelifes/tres/_pdf/ko_eamm12.pdf)  [https://www.pnas.org/doi/10.1073/pnas.1306390110](https://www.pnas.org/doi/10.1073/pnas.1306390110)   |
| Why BlsA is not suitable | The initial selection of BslA (YuaB) as a surface-display candidate was based on data from Kim et al. (2021). Their study showed that expressing a YuaB-His fusion via a strong P43 promoter in the pSDJH100 vector yielded positive fluorescent antibody signals on both intact cells and wall-less protoplasts. Because the signal survived cell-wall digestion, the authors concluded that YuaB acts as a permanent transmembrane stake that roots into the plasma membrane and protrudes out through the cell envelope. However, reconciling this with foundational studies by Kobayashi and Iwano (2012) and Hobley et al. (2013) reveals that this anchoring mechanism is a laboratory artifact. Natively, wild-type BslA is a fully secreted, soluble monomer with no biochemical affinity or binding motifs for peptidoglycan. The apparent "membrane retention" seen in the vector assay is actually the result of **expression saturation**. Overproducing this highly hydrophobic protein via an ultra-strong promoter overwhelms the cell's native secretion and signal-cleavage machinery. This causes the emerging proteins to jam and aggregate at the translocon-membrane interface. What appeared to be a structural transmembrane anchor was simply a congested molecular pileup trapped at the cell boundary. **Technical Debate and Gene Identity Summary** BslA and YuaB are the exact same gene encoding a single protein with a dual phenotype. In nature, it possesses a cleavable signal peptide and is entirely secreted to form a communal hydrophobic biofilm shield (Hobley et al., 2013; Ostrowski et al., 2011; Kobayashi & Iwano, 2012). Conversely, when overexpressed in specialized vectors like pSDJH100, it can be artificially forced to jam into the plasma membrane (Kim et al., 2021). Despite this engineered possibility, a systematic review of the literature confirms that BslA/YuaB is fundamentally unsuitable for a Carbonic Anhydrase (CA) project and must be eliminated. **Core Reasons for the Elimination of BslA (YuaB) Biofilm Matrix and EPS Dependency:** Natively, BslA lacks a peptidoglycan-binding domain. Kobayashi & Iwano (2012) proved that in a mutant strain lacking the exopolysaccharide operon ($\\Delta eps$), BslA fails to bind the cell and completely diffuses away into the liquid medium. Relying on an unstable extracellular matrix rather than a fixed, direct cell-wall anchor makes individual cell localization highly variable. **Mass Crystalline Entombment and Starvation:** BslA naturally migrates to the outermost fluid-air periphery of a colony to form a hydrophobic "raincoat" (Kobayashi & Iwano, 2012). Fusing CA to BslA concentrates your enzyme at the outer boundary of the biofilm. During calcification, rapid mineral growth will occur directly within this outer skin, creating a solid calcium carbonate ($CaCO\_3$) crust that entombs the biofilm, blocks nutrient transport, and suffocates the colony (Wilking et al., 2013). **Incompatible Enzyme Exposure Geometry:** In the alternative state where YuaB remains bound to the cell, it functions as a tight transmembrane stake pinned to the plasma membrane (Kim et al., 2021). Because its evolutionary role is to stitch structural layers together, it remains buried deeply inside the narrow channels of the dense cell envelope. High-efficiency carbon capture requires the enzyme to project prominently into the fluid boundary layer. The deep, rigid topology of YuaB depresses catalytic kinetics compared to dedicated cell wall-binding modules. **Unwanted Cellular Aggregation and Clumping:** Overproducing the YuaB anchor alters native phenotypes, forcing recombinant bacteria to heavily self-aggregate into tight cellular clumps (Kim et al., 2021). For a sand-stabilization project, you require a uniform, individual cell suspension capable of migrating through porous sand matrices. Macroscopic clumping blocks pore spaces prematurely and chokes fluid circulation, resulting in fractured and inconsistent calcification throughout the soil profile. |  | **Kim et al., 2021** (*Biotechnology and Bioprocess Engineering*, 10.1007/s12257-020-0397-7) – Notes YuaB protoplast localization and demonstrates that overexpression induces massive bacterial self-aggregation. **Kobayashi & Iwano, 2012** (*Molecular Microbiology*, 10.1111/j.1365-2958.2012.08094.x / PMC3165672) – Establishes the BslA/YuaB identity, defines its localization at the outer biofilm periphery, and proves its absolute dependency on an EPS matrix. **Hobley et al., 2013** (*PNAS*, 10.1073/pnas.1306390110) – Details the dynamic conformational changes and hydrophobic cap mechanism required for BslA sheet assembly.  |
| Spore Coat (CotE, cotB, etc.) | This study expressed a color-degenerating enzyme on a Bassilus Spore. We can theoretically substitute the gene to CA and it should work. Here are possible issues/changes:  We cannot use the paper's exact pH 9.0 Tris-HCl matrix blindly. We will have to run a pH profile matrix (testing across pH 6.0 to 8.5) using standard CA assay buffers. The Trace Metal Additives. Section 2.2 of the paper explicitly dictates adding an iron, manganese, and calcium cocktail to the Schaeffer’s sporulation medium. Because Carbonic Anhydrase relies fundamentally on Zinc, you should supplement the medium with a sterile micro-concentration of Zinc Chloride or Zinc Sulfate to ensure the enzyme gets its structural cofactor during spore coat crystallization. The paper fused the 37 kDa SLAC gene to the C-terminal of the 20.8 kDa CotE spore coat protein. Carbonic Anhydrases (CA) vary in size (e.g., human CA II is 29–30 kDa), and a bulky or fast-folding CA can physically block spore coat assembly or become covered and inactivated by CotE. If your CA loses activity or fails to display, change the paper's standard flexible linker (GGGGS) to a longer one like (GGGGS)3, or a rigid alpha-helical linker like EAAAK to push the enzyme away from the spore surface. Summarized Method (Paper):  **Sourcing the Bacteria and Genes:** The researchers started with two types of bacteria: E. coli (used as a biological factory to build and manipulate DNA) and a specially modified Bacillus subtilis (used as the host to display the final enzyme).  They extracted the DNA of another bacteria, Streptomyces coelicolor, to isolate a specific gene called SLAC (small laccase), which produces an enzyme capable of degrading dyes.  **Plasmid Construction:** Using a technique called PCR (Polymerase Chain Reaction), they amplified the SLAC gene.  They designed a combined genetic sequence so that the SLAC enzyme would be physically fused to CotE, a protein that naturally forms the tough outer coat of B. subtilis spores. \* They also added a tiny chemical marker called a "His6 tag" to the end of the sequence so they could easily track and detect the enzyme later.  This combined DNA sequence was inserted into a circular piece of DNA (a plasmid) called pSDJH100.   **Transforming the Host Bacteria:** The newly built plasmid containing the CotE-SLAC genetic instructions was inserted into the living B. subtilis host bacteria.   **Culturing and Spore Formation:** The modified B. subtilis bacteria were grown in a specific liquid nutrient mixture called Schaeffer’s medium, which was supplemented with trace metals (iron, manganese, and calcium).  They were incubated in a shaking flask at 37°C for 24 hours. This specific environment stresses the bacteria, forcing them to transition from actively growing cells into dormant, highly durable spores. As the spores form, the CotE-SLAC enzyme naturally anchors itself to the exterior surface of the spore coat.   **Purifying and Verifying the Spores:** The cells were harvested, and the pure spores were isolated using a chemical density gradient method (Renografin).  To prove that the SLAC enzyme was truly facing the outside of the spore, they coated the spores with a fluorescent, glowing antibody that specifically attaches to the "His6 tag". They then ran the spores through a laser-scanning machine called a flow cytometer to confirm they were glowing, which verified successful surface display.  |  | [https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9798364/](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9798364/)  |
