| Covalent Anchoring of Carbonic Anhydrase on *Bacillus subtilis* via Sortase Technology [Edward Chen](mailto:sec9064@nyu.edu) |  **1\. (Most promising method) The Sortase-Mediated Anchoring Mechanism** The most effective method for **covalent anchoring** on *B. subtilis* involves the **sortase-mediated system**, specifically utilizing the **YhcS sortase** enzyme. To achieve surface display of CA, a fusion protein must be genetically engineered with two primary components: **N-terminal Signal Peptide:** To ensure the CA enzyme is secreted across the cytoplasmic membrane. **C-terminal Cell Wall-Binding Motif (CWM):** This motif must contain a specific recognition sequence, such as **LPDTS** ( ). The YhcS sortase recognizes this LPDTS motif, cleaves it, and **covalently links** the CA enzyme directly to the **peptidoglycan layer** of the bacterial cell wall. This creates a stable, site-specific attachment that ensures the enzyme remains firmly bound during industrial processing. ![][image8] **2\. Carbonic Anhydrase Functionality in *B. subtilis*** *B. subtilis* has already been successfully engineered to express recombinant CA from *Bacillus megaterium*. In these systems, CA acts as a catalyst for the **hydration of CO2**, which is a critical step in **microbially induced calcium carbonate precipitation (MICP)**. **Sequestration Efficiency:** Engineered CA in *B. subtilis* has demonstrated the ability to reduce CO2 concentrations from **3800 PPM to 820 PPM**. **Biomineralization:** The process converts sequestered CO2 into stable minerals like **calcite and vaterite**, which are valuable for producing **carbon-negative cementitious materials** in the construction industry. **3\. Advantages of Covalent Surface Display** Utilizing covalent anchoring via sortase technology offers several significant improvements over free or intracellular CA: **Enhanced Stability:** Covalent immobilization stabilizes the enzyme's active site, protecting it from thermal stress and pH fluctuations. For instance, similar sortase-anchored enzymes have retained high activity at temperatures where free enzymes denature. **High Reusability:** Covalent systems effectively **eliminate enzyme leaching**, allowing the whole-cell biocatalyst to be recycled. In similar models, catalysts retained **50% of their activity after nine reuse cycles**, significantly reducing operational costs. **Unrestricted Substrate Access:** Unlike encapsulation, which creates diffusion barriers, **surface display** exposes the CA directly to the reaction medium (CO2 and calcium sources), maximizing catalytic efficiency. **Simplified Production:** This "whole-cell" approach eliminates the need for expensive **enzyme purification** or chemical cross-linking, as the living cells act as both the production factory and the carrier. **4\. Biosafety and Industrial Feasibility** Using *B. subtilis* as the host for CA anchoring is ideal for industrial applications because it is a **Generally Recognized as Safe (GRAS)** organism with a non-pathogenic profile. **Scalability:** *B. subtilis* can be grown to **high cell densities** in standard bioreactors, facilitating large-scale production of the anchored CA biocatalyst. **Storage:** Covalently anchored enzymes on *B. subtilis* exhibit excellent **long-term storage stability**, maintaining significant functionality for up to eight weeks when stored at 4°C.  |  | https://2017.igem.org/Team:SZU-China/Design |
| Covalent Method \- Sortase technology | **Paper: Thermostable and recyclable Candida antarctica lipase B immobilized on Bacillus subtilis using sortase technology**  Advantage of using living cells: Living cells act as both carriers and catalysts, offering biocompatibility, ease of scaling, and enhanced enzyme protection. Brief explanation on the technology: Sortase-mediated immobilization on gram-positive bacteria uses sortase enzymes to covalently attach proteins to the bacterial cell wall (thick peptidoglycan layer).  Genetic Construct Design:  N-terminal signal peptide for secretion (AmyQ to target it to the Sec pathway) (SpA) Enzyme of interest Flexible linker C-terminal cell wall sorting signal which comprises: a recognition motif (LPDTS in B. subtilis) a hydrophobic region a charged tail  Sortase: Sortase enzymes, such as YhcS in B. subtilis, cleave the sorting motif and covalently link the protein to the peptidoglycan layer, ensuring stable immobilization Bacillus subtilis codes for two putative sortases: YhcS and YwpE (discard YwpE because experimental evidence demonstrates it plays no functional role as a sortase, and shows no detectable natural gene expression, whereas YhcS is the enzyme responsible for successfully anchoring target proteins to the cell wall in *B. subtilis*)\[[3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3222306/)\] two surface proteins, YhcR and YfkN (choose **YhcR** and discard **YfkN** because when fused to a reporter enzyme, the YhcR sorting sequence successfully displayed high amounts of recombinant protein on the cell surface, whereas the YfkN sequence could hardly be detected.)\[[3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3222306/)\] How it works inside the cell: Once the fusion protein is secreted, the native membrane-bound sortase enzyme (YhcS) recognizes the LPDTS motif, cleaves it between the Threonine and Serine, and covalently attaches your enzyme directly to the cross-bridges of the B. subtilis peptidoglycan cell wall. \[1\] Substrate proteins are not directly transferred to the cell wall, but to the peptidoglycan intermediate lipid II. \[[3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3222306/)\] ![][image9] ![][image10]\[[4](https://doi.org/10.1042/BA20040014)\] Before this paper was published, researchers knew that *Bacillus subtilis* 168 possessed genes for potential sortases and surface proteins, but nobody had experimentally proven how they worked or whether they could be hijacked to display engineered proteins.\[[3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3222306/)\] Vector used: pHY300-PLK Media and growth conditions: super-rich medium (SRM) containing Bacto tryptose (25 g/L), yeast extract (20 g/L), dipotassium phosphate (3 g/L), and glucose (4.5 g/L) at pH 7.5. Cultures were supplemented with 25 µg/mL tetracycline and incubated at 30°C for 14 hours before harvesting for downstream analyses. Evaluating/assessment of the protein of interest: whole-cell enzyme activity assay thermal stability and denaturation profile pH profile against control Evaluation of continuous activity over 24 hours Reusability long-term storage stability Validation of cell wall-anchored PoI expression on Bacillus subtilis surface via SDS-PAGE as well as quantification Future work: Future work will explore CRISPR-Cas9-mediated chromosomal integration of the CALB gene to combat plasmid instability in large-scale fermentations | Useful language for wiki about the advantages of this technology | [https://doi.org/10.1128/spectrum.01121-25](https://doi.org/10.1128/spectrum.01121-25) [https://journals.asm.org/doi/10.1128/jb.00100-25\#:\~:text=Upon%20membrane%20translocation%2C%20surface%20protein,cell%20wall%20peptidoglycan%20(41)](https://journals.asm.org/doi/10.1128/jb.00100-25#:~:text=Upon%20membrane%20translocation%2C%20surface%20protein,cell%20wall%20peptidoglycan%20\(41\)). This paper is a general overview paper [https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3222306/](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3222306/) [https://doi.org/10.1042/BA20040014](https://doi.org/10.1042/BA20040014)  |
| Sortase technology in bacillus subtilis [Edward Chen](mailto:sec9064@nyu.edu) | Sortase enzyme to be used: YhcS  Potentially belongs to the group SrtD sortases expressed at a higher level during the late stationary phase Corresponding substrate: YhcR N-terminal end (residues 1 to 46): contains a signal peptide that is predicted to direct secretion by the twin-arginine translocation(TAT) pathway  C-terminal end: typical Gram-positive anchor For structural [info](https://journals.asm.org/doi/10.1128/jb.186.16.5376-5383.2004) putative sorting signals: LPDTS YhcR123: 123-amino-acid spacer derived from YhcR (amyQ signal peptide —CA1— linker —CA2 — 50-aa designer linker — 102-aa natural spacer — sorting motif until T — meso-DAP) Carbonic Anhydrase Monomer 1\] \-\> \[Central Flexible Linker Loop\] \-\> \[Carbonic Anhydrase Monomer 2\] \-\> \[50-amino-acid Designer Linker\] \-\> \[102-amino-acid Natural Spacer\] \-\> \[Leucine-Proline-Aspartate-Threonine (LPDT)\] \-\> (Covalent Isopeptide Amide Bond) \-\> \[Side-chain epsilon-amino group of meso-DAP\] \-\> \[Peptidoglycan Stem Peptide (Position 3)\] \-\> \[C-Terminus Bound to Sugar Backbone\] Concern:  YhcS has been classified in group SrtD sortases, but there is no clear experimental evidence that class SrtD sortases recognize and anchor proteins on the surface of Gram-positive bacteria They describe it as “sortase-like” established a [system](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0168165605006218?via%3Dihub) to immobilize proteins on the surface of a B. subtilis strain expressing L. monocytogenes srtA Regarding acceptor anchoring peptide: The *B. subtilis* cell wall contains peptide crosslinks identical to those present in the *L. monocytogenes* cell walls. This suggests that the crosslink of potential surface proteins to the peptidoglycan is formed by the nucleophilic attack of the amino group of m-diaminopimelic acid cross-bridge within the lipid II precursor as in the case of *L. monocytogenes*. To display: a linker of ∼123 amino acids is sufficient to penetrate through the thick B. subtilis cell wall and project the reporter to the cell [surface](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0168165605006218?pes=doc&utm_source=asm&getft_integrator=asm) |  | [https://link.springer.com/article/10.1186/s12934-021-01701-4](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1186/s12934-021-01701-4)  |
| Comparing gene constructs across sortase papers |  Research Paper Bacterial strain Sortase N-terminal Signal Seq Protein of interest C-terminal sorting seq Acceptor anchor protein (?) [1](https://journals.asm.org/doi/10.1128/jb.05711-11) WB800 yhcS SacB signal peptide (SP) β-lactamase reporter 152-amino-acid linker, CWADYhcR   \* first 50 amino acids \= designer linker \* 102 amino acids \= naturally present upstream of the LPDTS sorting motif of CWADYhcR  \* CWADYhcR  \= LPDTS sorting motif, a transmembrane segment, and a positively charged tail [2](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3222306/) WB800 YhcS AmyQ signal peptide α-Amylase 123-amino-acids spacer, yhcR sorting sequence (36 amino acids) Outdated. Paper 1: more modern linker design. [3](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1186/s12934-021-01701-4) WASD YhcS Native signal peptide of spA. (both B. subtilis and S. aureus use Sec pathway) SpA cell wall motif (CWM) of YhcR  |  | \[1\][https://journals.asm.org/doi/10.1128/jb.05711-11](https://journals.asm.org/doi/10.1128/jb.05711-11)   \[2\][https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3222306/](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3222306/)  \[3\][https://link.springer.com/article/10.1186/s12934-021-01701-4](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1186/s12934-021-01701-4)   |
| Spores vs vegetative state | Protein folding issues associated with unfolded polypeptide chains crossing membranes are circumvented with spores \[1\]. ![][image11]  |  | [https://doi.org/10.3390/microorganisms12010097](https://doi.org/10.3390/microorganisms12010097) [https://doi.org/10.1186/s13068-025-02635-4](https://doi.org/10.1186/s13068-025-02635-4) |
| Transformation Protocols for *B. Subtilis* 168 [Edward Chen](mailto:sec9064@nyu.edu) | [https://2009.igem.org/Team:Newcastle/Project/Labwork/OurProtocols/TransformBac](https://2009.igem.org/Team:Newcastle/Project/Labwork/OurProtocols/TransformBac) [https://2022.igem.wiki/imperial-college-london/protocols](https://2022.igem.wiki/imperial-college-london/protocols) **"Construction of a Super-Competent Bacillus subtilis 168 Using the PmtlA-comKS Inducible Cassette"**  **1\. Background & Rationale** *Bacillus subtilis* is a major model organism in biotechnology, widely appreciated for its ability to achieve "natural competence"—a state where it can uptake extracellular DNA directly from its environment. However, traditional transformation methods (like the 50-year-old Spizizen method) are highly inefficient, time-consuming, and require strict, nutrient-poor minimal media. Even at peak conditions, only a tiny subpopulation ($\\approx 10\\%$) of cells transitions into a competent state when entering the stationary phase. The transition into competence is master-regulated by the transcription factor **ComK**. Another protein, **ComS**, plays a critical role in preventing ComK from being degraded. **2\. Experimental Objective** The authors aimed to develop a faster, highly efficient transformation method for *B. subtilis* 168 that functions well in **rich media** (like LB broth) by bypassing the natural, complex regulatory networks and directly driving the expression of key competence genes. **3\. Methodology & Strain Construction** To engineer artificial competence, the researchers evaluated inserting different combinations of competence genes (comK, comS, and dprA) into the *B. subtilis* 168 chromosome. **The Inducible Promoter:** They placed these genes under the control of the **PmtlA promoter**, which is tightly regulated and strongly induced by the addition of **mannitol**. **Strain Development (REG19):** Through testing, the combination of overexpressing both comK **and** comS together yielded the highest transformation efficiency. The engineered strain containing this **PmtlA-comKS** cassette was named **REG19**. **4\. Key Findings Massive Increase in Efficiency:** Upon adding mannitol, the transformation efficiency of the REG19 strain using plasmid DNA increased **6.7-fold** compared to the wild-type strain 168\. **Rapid Competence Induction:** Unlike the native pathway which takes several hours and complex media shifts, the artificial system achieved maximal competence after just **1.5 hours of mannitol induction** in rich media. **DNA Saturation Thresholds:** The transformability of REG19 cells reached a saturation plateau in the presence of 100 ng of dimeric plasmid DNA or 3000 ng of chromosomal DNA. **Independence from Global Regulators:** Because the cassette overrode the complex native transcription cascade, important upstream global regulators typically required for competence (such as Spo0A, ComQXPA, and DegU) could be entirely deleted without stopping the strain from becoming competent. **Dependence on Endogenous Genes:** Interestingly, despite having the synthetic **PmtlA-comKS** cassette, efficient transformation still relied on the original, native chromosomal copies of comK and comS being intact. **5\. Application and Significance** To demonstrate the practical value of this "super-competent" strain, the authors integrated it with **Gibson assembly** protocols. They successfully showed that researchers can perform multi-fragment DNA assemblies *in vitro* and transform them directly into *B. subtilis* REG19 without needing a plasmid vector or an intermediate cloning step in *E. coli*. **Conclusion** The paper successfully delivers a streamlined, reliable genetic tool. By shifting competence induction from a delicate physiological response to a simple chemical trigger (mannitol) in rich media, the engineered strain REG19 significantly reduces the time and complexity required for genetic engineering in *Bacillus subtilis*.  |  | [10.3389/fmicb.2015.01431](https://doi.org/10.3389/fmicb.2015.01431)   |
| Previous applications of sortase mediated anchoring in iGEM context and the results | [https://2023.igem.wiki/ubc-okanagan/engineering/](https://2023.igem.wiki/ubc-okanagan/engineering/) [https://2019.igem.org/Team:TU\_Darmstadt/Project/Sortase](https://2019.igem.org/Team:TU_Darmstadt/Project/Sortase)  |  |  |
| Do we need to co-express sortase? | Short answer: yeah “Inactivation of yhcS abolished the cell wall anchoring reaction. The amounts of fusion protein anchored to the cell wall were proportional to the levels of YhcS.” \[1\] \-\> more sortase \= more anchorage “Furthermore, yhcS is not essential for the survival of B. subtilis under the cultivation condition tested” \[1\] \-\> this means that the bacteria will not be naturally drawn to produce huge amounts of sortase. “Sortase can be co-expressed for better results.” \[2\] previous iGEM team. “The ChiS fusion protein is displayed at the cell wall surface when YhcS is co-expressed. In the absence of YhcS, or when no cell-wall-anchoring motif is fused to ChiS, the ChiS accumulates predominately in the culture medium” \[3\] “The gene encoding YhcR is in the same operon as the gene encoding the putative sortase YhcS, suggesting that they are functionally related.” \[3\] \-\> this also means that they are naturally co-expressed and it's usually a good idea to repeat what’s natural. Also, all papers we have been inspired by are co-expressing sortase.  | \[1\][https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3256611/](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3256611/)  \[2\][https://2023.igem.wiki/ubc-okanagan/engineering/](https://2023.igem.wiki/ubc-okanagan/engineering/)  \[3\][https://analyticalsciencejournals.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/pmic.201100174](https://analyticalsciencejournals.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/pmic.201100174)  |  |
| Testing the localization of CA | Subcellular Fractionation & Western Blotting  Inspired by Chen et al. (2008) & Nguyen et al. (2011). Uses centrifuge and lysozymes. Requires, among others, electro-blotting apparatus (we have it at NYUAD but not sure if we have access) and specific reagents.  Procedure: 4 different solutions are tested:  **Media Fraction (M):** Centrifuge the culture at 5,000g. Save the liquid supernatant. This contains any unanchored, escaped CA. **Cell Wall Fraction (CW):** Take the remaining cell pellet and resuspend it in a **hypertonic buffer containing 0.5M–1.0M sucrose** (to keep cells from bursting) and **lysozyme**. Incubate at 37°C until cells turn into spherical protoplasts. Centrifuge at 7,000g. **The supernatant is your Cell Wall fraction**, containing your cleared CA. **Cytoplasmic (C) and Membrane (MB) Fractions:** Take the protoplast pellet and resuspend it in a **hypotonic buffer** (no sucrose). The cells will osmotically pop. Ultracentrifuge at 100,000g. The supernatant is your **Cytoplasm (C)**; the remaining pellet is your **Membrane (MB)**. Western Blotting performed for these 4 lanes and controls:  **Phase 1: Sample Preparation & Denaturation** Total protein concentrations across all cell fractions (Media, Cell Wall, Cytoplasm, and Membrane) are quantified via BCA assay and equalized 20–30mug per lane). Samples are mixed with a Laemmli loading buffer containing SDS and beta-mercaptoethanol to disrupt the complex tertiary structure and zinc-binding coordinates of the CA enzyme. The multi-domain, high-molecular-weight hybrid proteins (**AmyQ-CA1-Linker-CA2-Spacer-LPDTS**) are fully unfolded and linearized by boiling the mixture at 95°C–100°C for 5–10 minutes. **Phase 2: Gel Electrophoresis (SDS-PAGE)** Denatured samples and a pre-stained molecular weight ladder are loaded into the wells of a polyacrylamide gel. An electric current is applied (80V through the stacking gel, upgraded to 120V–150V through the resolving gel) to separate proteins based strictly on molecular weight. Because the dual-CA hybrid construct possesses a significantly higher molecular weight than native *B. subtilis* proteins, electrophoresis is halted once the tracking dye reaches the gel base to maximize resolution in the upper kilodalton (kDa) range. **Phase 3: Protein Transfer (Blotting)** A transfer sandwich is assembled under buffer, layering the gel directly against an activated PVDF or nitrocellulose membrane between filter papers and foam pads. Air bubbles are strictly removed to prevent current distortion. An electric field (100V for 1–2 hours) is applied horizontally, forcing the negatively charged CA hybrid proteins out of the gel matrix and binding them onto the membrane surface. **Phase 4: Blocking** To prevent non-specific antibody binding, the membrane is incubated in a blocking solution of 5% non-fat dry milk or BSA in TBST. The solution is agitated with gentle rocking for 1 hour at room temperature to coat any remaining blank spaces on the membrane. **Phase 5: Antibody Incubation** The membrane is incubated overnight at 4°C with a highly specific primary antibody targeting either the CA enzyme directly or the engineered epitope tag (e.g., anti-His) embedded in the designer linker. Unbound primary antibodies are removed via 3–5 automated washes with TBST. The membrane is incubated with an HRP-conjugated secondary antibody for 1 hour at room temperature to target the host species of the primary antibody. Excess secondary antibody is cleared with a final round of TBST washes. **Phase 6: Imaging and Analysis** Enhanced Chemiluminescence (ECL) substrate is applied evenly across the membrane surface. The resulting chemiluminescent signal, emitted by HRP substrate cleavage, is captured using a digital imaging system or X-ray film. Protein bands are analyzed and mapped against the molecular weight ladder to confirm target identity and relative abundance per fraction. Successful surface display is confirmed if the target CA band appears predominantly within the Cell Wall fraction. Warning: To use Anti-His-Tag Antibody, our construct should include a C-terminal histidine tail. To use anti-CBM3, our construct should include CBM3 (Carbohydrate-Binding Module) tag.  Esterase Activity Assay using p-Nitrophenyl Acetate (pNPA) (not testing localization, but tests functionality) [https://pdf.benchchem.com/12414/Colorimetric\_Assays\_for\_Carbonic\_Anhydrase\_Activity\_Application\_Notes\_and\_Protocols.pdf](https://pdf.benchchem.com/12414/Colorimetric_Assays_for_Carbonic_Anhydrase_Activity_Application_Notes_and_Protocols.pdf)  This is the most common colorimetric assay for carbonic anhydrase. It relies on the ability of CA to hydrolyze the ester substrate p-nitrophenyl acetate (pNPA) to produce the chromophoric product p-nitrophenol, which can be quantified spectrophotometrically by measuring the increase in absorbance at 400-405 nm.  ![][image12] Materials: Carbonic Anhydrase (purified enzyme or biological sample) p-Nitrophenyl Acetate (pNPA) Assay Buffer (e.g., 50 mM Tris-SO4, pH 7.6) Acetonitrile or DMSO (for dissolving pNPA) Microplate reader or spectrophotometer capable of measuring absorbance at 405 nm 96-well microplate (clear, flat-bottom) Procedure (approx.): Aliquot washed, intact *B. subtilis* whole cells into a 96-well plate. Add a freshly prepared solution of *p*\-NPA dissolved in a phosphate buffer (pH 7.0–7.5). Immediately place the plate in a spectrophotometer and read the absorbance at **405 nm** continuously every 15–30 seconds for 5 to 10 minutes. This will report on activity of CA, but since pNPA can enter the cell, it is less relevant for localisation.  |  |  |
| Using a simpler experiment to confirm the method | What is the problem with ours?  There are many steps that must work correctly. We should make sure that 1\) sortase anchoring mechanism is functional and can attach proteins to the cell wall via sorting signal and linkers we use in our construct; 2\) the linker in between two CA enzymes allow them to form a functional dimer.  I suggest we use a simpler experiment without the transformation of alive *B. subtilis* cells to answer each of these concerns.  Confirming Sortase mediated anchoring functionality Attaching GFP to L-Lysine or D-Lysine coated beads (or plate) This suggestion is inspired by a following paper: [https://pubs.acs.org/doi/10.1021/bc060339w](https://pubs.acs.org/doi/10.1021/bc060339w)  Instead of immediately trying the construct on a live cell, we will first confirm that the Sortase gene which we are expressing is functional and that the construct we want to use for CA anchoring works well with sortase. For this experiment there is no functional difference between L-Lysine or D-Lysine coated plate, because no proteases and no live cells will be used.  The Logic: PDL/PLL presents a dense, positively charged matrix exposing hundreds of repeating primary aliphatic amines epsilon-NH2 side chains). Because YhcS naturally transfers surface proteins to the structurally similar primary amine on the D-center of meso-diaminopimelic acid (meso-DAP), these D-lysine side chains serve as highly accessible, biomimetic nucleophiles For this experiment I suggest we use following GFP constructs: NH2— eGFP — 50-aa designer linker — 102-aa natural spacer — sorting motif LPDTS  — charged tail— HHHHHH— COOH (what we want to use for CA-linker-CA expression, based on other studies) NH2— eGFP— LPDTS— HHHHHH— COOH (what this study used for sortase anchoring experiments) Comparing these will give an insight on the difference presence or absence of the long linkers makes on the function of sortase. On the other hand, anchoring a protein on the glass is not exactly same as anchoring it on the cell surface, and we would need a long linker regardless of the result of this experiment, for CA to be able to float away from the cell, within the surface of biofilm, to catalyse the MICP reaction. Yet, there are multiple possible ways to construct this long linker and I believe that checking this one against a simpler version can give us useful information on its functionality and effectiveness.  The H6 tag is useful for purification of GFP. eGFP is enhanced GFP for better visualization, but regular GFP is also ok.  I believe for the most part we can copy the method given in the paper. Approximate methodology summarized by Gemini: “**Phase 1: Protein Preparation (The Standard Stuff) Express and Purify your targets** in *E. coli* using standard Immobilized Metal Affinity Chromatography (IMAC/Nickel columns) via your $His\_6$ tags. **Tube A:** Your purified YhcS (Sortase D) enzyme. **Tube B:** Your short control eGFP-LPDTS-His cargo. **Tube C:** Your long engineered eGFP-Linker-Spacer-LPDTS-His cargo. Quantify your protein concentrations (via Bradford or NanoDrop) so you can match molar ratios. **Phase 2: The Immobilization Reaction** *Note: Sortase D reactions do not require calcium, but they are notoriously slow compared to standard Sortase A. Give them time.* **Block the Wells/Beads:** Poly-D-Lysine is incredibly sticky. To prevent the GFP from randomly binding to exposed plastic, block your PDL-coated plate wells (or a tube of PDL beads) with a standard **1% BSA (Bovine Serum Albumin) in PBS** solution for 1 hour at room temperature. Wash once with PBS. **Set up your Reactions:** In your blocked PDL wells (or tubes containing aliquots of beads), add your reaction components. A standard sortase reaction buffer is **50mM Tris-HCl (pH 7.5) and 150mM NaCl**. **Pipette the groups: Well/Tube 1 (Negative Control):** Reaction Buffer \+ Cargo Protein only (No YhcS). *This measures your background baseline.* **Well/Tube 2 (Short Construct):** Reaction Buffer \+ YhcS \+ Short eGFP-LPDTS. **Well/Tube 3 (Long Construct):** Reaction Buffer \+ YhcS \+ Long eGFP-Linker-Spacer-LPDTS. **Incubate:** Seal the plate or tubes to prevent evaporation. Incubate at **30C to 37C overnight (12–18 hours)** on a gentle shaker or rocker. **Phase 3: The Critical High-Salt Wash** This is where you strip away the "static cling" (electrostatic binding) of the unreacted GFP to the positively charged lysine matrix. Aspirate the overnight reaction mixture. Wash each well or pellet the beads and wash **3 to 5 times** using a **High-Salt Wash Buffer 50mM Tris-HCl pH 7.5 containing 500mM to 1M NaCl**. Let the high-salt buffer sit in the wells for 2–3 minutes during each wash step to thoroughly break up any non-covalent ionic bonds between the GFP and the Poly-D-Lysine. Do a final rinse with standard PBS to remove excess salt before reading. **Phase 4: Analysis If using a 96-well Plate Reader:** Add 100muL of PBS to the wells. Read the green fluorescence on a plate reader set to **Excitation: 488nm / Emission: 510nm**. **If using Microbeads:** Resuspend your beads in PBS and run them through a **Flow Cytometer** or place a drop on a slide under a **Fluorescence Microscope**.” We can also use plates which will be easier for data analysis, but it will require an extra step to block non-specific links. We can also just buy the L-Lysine or D-Lysine coated plates, but pre-made beads seem to be less available. “The Plate (PDL): A Poly-D-Lysine plate is a flat plastic well coated with a highly sticky, positively charged polymer chain. The plastic underneath is notorious for non-specific protein adsorption, which is why BSA is mandatory. The Beads: When you buy or prepare functionalized beads (like those used in the paper), the Lysine or Glycine residues are covalently welded directly to the bead matrix via tight chemical links. There is no messy, highly charged polymer floating around to trap random proteins.” (Gemini) Confirming CA-linker-CA functionality Expressing in E. coli and conducting Esterase Activity Assay using p-Nitrophenyl Acetate (pNPA)\\ I suggest we use available vectors from the providers for E. coli and express CA-linker-CA through the simplest possible pathway. Then lyse the cells and conduct an Esterase Activity Assay as described in the previous section. |  |  |
| Using **Phenol-red CO2 hydration assay** to determine CA functionality [Naya Mansour](mailto:nm5098@nyu.edu) | **What it is:** A colorimetric test where phenol red (a pH indicator) changes color from pink to yellow as CO₂ gets hydrated into bicarbonate \+ H⁺. Since CA catalyzes exactly this reaction, active CA makes the color change happen fast; no CA (or dead/inhibited CA) makes it slow. **Chemical principle:** CA catalyzes **CO₂ \+ H₂O ⇌ HCO₃⁻ \+ H⁺**. This reaction is slow uncatalyzed but sped up dramatically by CA. Each turnover releases a proton, so as CO₂ gets hydrated, the solution acidifies. Phenol red (pKa \~7.9) is pink/red above pH \~8.2 and yellow below \~6.8, so its color directly tracks this pH drop —\> fast acidification (active CA) \= fast color shift. **Why I'm suggesting it:** The team's planned pNPA assay only tests CA's esterase side-activity, not its actual job (hydrating CO₂). A construct could pass pNPA but still be bad at real CO₂ hydration \= a false PoC. This assay tests the real reaction directly, so it's a better (or at least complementary) functional readout for our actual goal of CO₂ capture/MICP. **Brief protocol:** Resuspend CA⁺ and CA⁻ (control) cells in a weak phenol red buffer at pH \~8.2, add CO₂-saturated water, and time/measure how fast the color shifts to yellow. Faster shift \= more CA activity. Include a heat-killed and/or acetazolamide-inhibited control to confirm the signal is really CA-driven and not just cell respiration.  | I suggest doing first the pNpA experiment but with a control to test if we need to do this experiment additionally: **Acetazolamide-inhibited pNPA control.** Acetazolamide is a sulfonamide that binds directly in CA's zinc active site and blocks its catalytic mechanism: it inhibits both the esterase (pNPA) reaction *and* the true CO₂ hydration reaction, because it shuts down the same active site both rely on.  | [https://www.mdpi.com/2073-4344/12/11/1391](https://www.mdpi.com/2073-4344/12/11/1391)  [https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/epdf/10.3109/14756366.2013.868599?needAccess=true](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/epdf/10.3109/14756366.2013.868599?needAccess=true)   |
| Fully analysing Esterase Activity assay and phenol red assay: bullet proof approach |  |  |  |
| The Periplasmic Fractionation (in E. coli) | 1\) conceptual explanation and what information it will provide for the project:  We can use this method to confirm the functionality of the signal peptide we will use.  The idea was that *E. coli* and *B. subtilis* both use the Sec pathway so we should be able to confirm the functionality of a signal peptide in *E. coli.* Unfortunately, this is not true 🙁. Signal peptides of gram-positive exoproteins generally carry a higher net positive charge at their amino termini (N regions) and have longer hydrophobic cores (h regions) and carboxy termini (C regions) than do signal peptides of Escherichia coli envelope proteins. In the linked study, the signal peptides that work in *E. coli* were found to be ineffective in *B. subtilis*. There still is another way to try to test this.   |  | [https://journals.asm.org/doi/10.1128/jb.176.10.3013-3020.1994a](https://journals.asm.org/doi/10.1128/jb.176.10.3013-3020.1994a)  |
| LytE experiment More detailed procedure and analysis: [https://docs.google.com/document/d/1fzOwjBs1w5C09h38n68fA8cgn9kCSmei/edit?usp=sharing\&ouid=112698121198667832689\&rtpof=true\&sd=true](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1fzOwjBs1w5C09h38n68fA8cgn9kCSmei/edit?usp=sharing&ouid=112698121198667832689&rtpof=true&sd=true)  | **CWBMLytE–β-lactamase \+ nitrocefin colorimetric reporter CATEGORY:**  *Out of the cell — in vitro (also works as a whole-cell readout in Exp 5\)* **In one line — what it is & why it matters for us** Swap the real cargo (CA) for β-lactamase (Bla) — exactly the reporter used in the source display paper — and use the nitrocefin yellow→red color change as a fast, cheap check that an *anchored* protein is still folded, active, and accessible. It validates the anchor \+ linker \+ display concept before we sink time into CA-specific assays, and reproduces a published benchmark so we can trust our hands and constructs. **Conceptual summary** β-lactamase hydrolyzes the chromogenic β-lactam nitrocefin, shifting it from yellow (\~390 nm) to red (\~486 nm). If CWBMLytE–Bla is bound to PG (or cells), the bound fraction turns nitrocefin red while the anchor-only control stays yellow. The rate of color change scales with the amount of active, displayed enzyme. **Method (bench-ready)** Produce the reporter fusion, separate bound from unbound, then run a kinetic nitrocefin assay on each fraction. 1\.     Express and purify CWBMLytE–Bla in E. coli. 2\.     Either co-sediment with PG (as Exp 1\) or paint onto cells (Exp 5), then separate the bound fraction (pellet/cells) from the unbound (supernatant). 3\.     Resuspend fractions in PBS and add nitrocefin to \~50–100 µM final in a microplate. 4\.     Read A486 (and optionally A390) kinetically every 1–5 min in a plate reader; for a quick qualitative pass, simply watch the color. Calculate rate (ΔA486/min) and normalize to protein amount or cell number. **Controls & rigor:**  No-Bla (anchor-only) negative; free Bla positive; substrate-only blank for nitrocefin autohydrolysis; equal-input normalization across fractions. **Schematic ![][image13]** *Figure 3\. Bla on the bound fraction hydrolyzes nitrocefin (yellow→red); anchor-only and substrate-only stay pale. Rate of A486 rise \= active displayed enzyme.* **Possible outcomes — the good, the bad, and where it falls short** ·       **Positive result:**  The bound fraction develops red rapidly while anchor-only stays yellow → the displayed enzyme is folded, active and accessible, and the linker permits activity. Matching the paper’s benchmark means the method is validated in our hands. ·       **Negative / ambiguous:**  Little/no color in the bound fraction → the enzyme is inactive (fusion/linker misfold), inaccessible, or not bound; strong color only in the supernatant → active protein that failed to anchor. ·       **Limits & hurdles:**  A great Bla result confirms the display/anchor system works for a reporter — not that CA tolerates the same fusion, which must still be validated directly. Nitrocefin is light- and pH-sensitive and autohydrolyzes (blanks are essential), and whole-cell E. coli can give periplasmic Bla background (use purified protein or tight controls). | Nitrocefin is a chromogenic cephalosporin substrate that serves as a highly sensitive colorimetric reporter for detecting β-lactamase enzyme activity.  |  |
| Linker situation | What do we want to do? Linking CA to CA using one of the commonly used linkers. CA-linker-CA. We need CA to dimerize and be functional. This approach to engineered protein constructs has been around for more than 10 years. Linkers are used in nature to separate the domains of one protein from one another.  “Protein engineering techniques were another option to address unfolded or unstable proteins, using a single polypeptide chain chimera to link the two binding partners via a flexible amino acid linker13. With these chimeric proteins, it was then possible to maintain both the intramolecular and intermolecular protein–protein interactions,14 and chimeric proteins have been used to generate stable, soluble binary complexes for structural studies, as well as functional dimers.15” (1) So far to me it looks like a flexible linker will be preferred for CA-linker-CA construct.  |  | https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3588912/\#b15 |
| ASSays for CA or Adiya going crazy corner | **Wilbur–Anderson Assay:**  Very detailed masters thesis. She used e coli to produce SazCA, lysed and purified it. Conducted a WA assay to compare her creation to free SazCA. I think we should be able to copy the expression, purification and WA from her. But this is a masters thesis and not really a peer reviewed article so I will look for something better  [https://odr.chalmers.se/bitstreams/d846f911-5817-42af-9ac4-02c70814a754/download](https://odr.chalmers.se/bitstreams/d846f911-5817-42af-9ac4-02c70814a754/download)  Experiment with very specific SazCA isolation and WA assay: [https://link.springer.com/article/10.1186/s12934-024-02463-5\#Sec3](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1186/s12934-024-02463-5#Sec3)  Expressed in E. coli. C-terminal His-tagged.  ![][image14] TEV refers to the Tobacco Etch Virus protease cleavage site (sequence ENLYFQ/G, per the paper's Materials section). It's inserted between the His-tag and the CA coding sequence so that, after purification, TEV protease can cleave the tag off the enzyme at that specific sequence, leaving native (or near-native) CA protein without the 6xHis tag if it needs to be removed for downstream use. It's not part of the enzyme itself — just a cloning/purification convenience built into the construct. We can copy that, in case His-tag might interfere with the protein folding, etc. “The gene sequences were codon-optimized using E. coli codon usage frequency and synthesized by GenScript Inc for each of the seven constructs.”  So we should codon optimize the constructs, as they are taken from *Sulfurihydrogenibium azorense* and might be less effective in *E. coli*. We need dual-host codon optimization. It should be available in twist.  |  |  |
| Promoter — P43 [Sid Kong](mailto:zk2185@nyu.edu) | We use the P43 promoter — a strong, "always-on" *B. subtilis* promoter (from the *cdd* gene), already proven to overexpress this same operon (≥1.74 g/L γ-PGA). The iGEM-registry copy is only a 56 bp fragment, so we use the full **270 bp** version and confirmed it letter-for-letter against the *B. subtilis* 168 genome (it is the *cdd* promoter). | A reliable on-switch already proven to make γ-PGA, and we verified the sequence is the real thing. | Lin 2016 — [https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4814633/](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4814633/) · genome — [https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/nuccore/NC\_000964.3](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/nuccore/NC_000964.3) |
| RBS — capB [Sid Kong](mailto:zk2185@nyu.edu) | Kept the gene's own (native) ribosome binding site — the **30 bp** just before capB's start codon (Shine-Dalgarno core GAAGGAG). The **RBS Calculator** scored it strong, and it is an exact genome match. *(If we use the pHT01 vector, we use the vector's built-in RBS instead.)* | Used the gene's natural start signal and checked that it is strong. | RBS Calculator — [https://salislab.net/software/](https://salislab.net/software/) · genome (as above) |
| Coding sequence — capBCA operon [Sid Kong](mailto:zk2185@nyu.edu) | The 3 enzyme genes capB·capC·capA (= pgsBCA) from *B. subtilis* 168 (2807 bp) — enough on their own to make γ-PGA. We made **8 silent DNA edits** (proteins unchanged) so the part fits both BioBrick and Golden Gate standards. Note: in the genome they're under "cap" names, not "pgs." | The complete γ-PGA "machine"; tiny edits that don't change the proteins. | Urushibata 2002 — [https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC139567/](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC139567/) |
| Terminator — BBa\_B0015 [Sid Kong](mailto:zk2185@nyu.edu) | A standard "double" stop signal (129 bp). It's the intrinsic type that dominates termination in *B. subtilis*, so it works there. *(Measured mainly in E. coli; a B. subtilis-native terminator is an easy swap if we want one.)* | Stops transcription so it doesn't run into the plasmid; standard part, works in *B. subtilis*. | BBa\_B0015 — [https://parts.igem.org/Part:BBa\_B0015](https://parts.igem.org/Part:BBa_B0015) |
| Backbone (vector) — pBS0E vs pHT01 (deciding) [Sid Kong](mailto:zk2185@nyu.edu) | Original choice \= **pBS0E** (the only empty, replicative, BioBrick *B. subtilis* vector; **no signal peptide** — important, because our enzyme must stay inside the cell, so a secretion vector like pWB980 could break it). For the competition proof-of-concept we're reconsidering **pHT01** (commercial, easier to source) — but it is **inducible (needs IPTG), so lab-only**. **pBS0E \+ the constitutive P43 needs no inducer → better for eventual desert deployment.** None of the iGEM-kit plasmids work (they don't replicate in *B. subtilis*). | **Two tracks:** pHT01 for a fast PoC; keep **pBS0E \+ P43 for deployment** (always-on, no inducer). Decision depends on what we can source. | pBS0E (Popp 2017\) — [https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5678133/](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5678133/) · pHT01 — [https://www.mobitec.com/](https://www.mobitec.com/) |
| Host strain \+ build [Sid Kong](mailto:zk2185@nyu.edu) | Host \= *B. subtilis* **168** (= stock-centre strain "1A1" — same thing; any lab's 168 works). Build by simple BioBrick cut-and-paste (one insert into one vector), not Golden Gate. **De-risk:** γ-PGA is a polymer (can't carry a fluorescent tag), so we'll also build a control with the operon swapped for **sfGFP** — if cells glow, the backbone/promoter/RBS all work. | Any lab's 168 works; simple cloning; a "glow" control proves the system works. | BGSC 1A1 — [https://bgsc.org](https://bgsc.org/) · iGEM assembly — [https://parts.igem.org/Help:Standards/Assembly](https://parts.igem.org/Help:Standards/Assembly) |
|  |  |  |  |

# Sand Erosion R2

| Question | Info | Links |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| What factors influence calcium carbonate precipitation?  | Calcium carbonate precipitation is a rather straightforward chemical process governed mainly by four key factors:  (1) the calcium concentration, (2) the concentration of dissolved inorganic carbon (DIC), (3) the pH and (4) the availability of nucleation sites | \[1\] [https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S092585740900113X?via%3Dihub](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S092585740900113X?via%3Dihub)  |
| Paper Showdown questions: linker for lyte or not  N terminus vs c terminus |  Alphafold (linker vs no linker)→ relatively the same. Both with and without the first linker, structures mostly have the same pTM (meaning similarity between the output and the hypothetical true structure is mostly the same). Without linker is slightly higher, but that is because linkers themselves lower pTM (they are meant to move around, so their exact position is unknown). By removing one linker, we increased the pTM slightly. So, they are essentially equals by Alphafold’s standards. Alphafold (C or N terminus) ChimeraX |  |
| Summer Experiments:  | Make a row below to write findings please. This is for assignments 1\. B. subtilis 168 growth in LB media \- someone fetch a protocol/find papers or teams who have done it [Naya Mansour](mailto:nm5098@nyu.edu) 2\. Growth curve to identify sporulation peak \- how do we find sporulation peak  [Naya Mansour](mailto:nm5098@nyu.edu) 3\. Isolation of spores \- papers that do it, what materials needed [Hoor Islam](mailto:hi2151@nyu.edu) 4\. Create carrier solution with right conditions, ions, and spores\* \- how do we create a carrier solution w/ all this [Udaya Pandit](mailto:up2052@nyu.edu) 5\. Freeze-dry carrier solution\* \- protocol/papers that do this [Edward Chen](mailto:sec9064@nyu.edu) [Ndeye Astou Ndiaye](mailto:nn2705@nyu.edu) 6\. Deploy (germinant)\* [Edward Chen](mailto:sec9064@nyu.edu)  |  |
| Growth curve of Bacillus subtilis \+ Growth of spores  [Naya Mansour](mailto:nm5098@nyu.edu) | [https://static.igem.org/mediawiki/2020/5/50/T--Brno\_Czech\_Republic--Contribution\_Handbook.pdf](https://static.igem.org/mediawiki/2020/5/50/T--Brno_Czech_Republic--Contribution_Handbook.pdf)  This is basically a “how to Bacillus Subtilis” guide. It’s a really good starting point if we ever get confused on anything  [https://static.igem.org/mediawiki/2016/7/74/T--UBonn\_HBRS--How-To-Bacillus-Subtilis.pdf](https://static.igem.org/mediawiki/2016/7/74/T--UBonn_HBRS--How-To-Bacillus-Subtilis.pdf) also how to b.subtilis  **Protocol to get the growth curve: Day 0: Overnight culture** Inoculate your culture of Bacillus subtilis in 4 mL LB medium. Let it grow overnight at 37°C, 200 rpm. **Day 1: Growth curve** Measure the OD600 of your overnight culture and dilute it in LB medium to an OD600 of around 0.1–0.2/mL in 10 mL LB. Incubate at 37°C, 200 rpm. Sample OD₆₀₀ every 30 minutes (always blank against sterile LB, and use the same cuvette/well each time to avoid variability). Continue sampling through lag phase, exponential phase, and into the plateau (stationary phase). Plot OD₆₀₀ (or log OD₆₀₀) vs. time. **What to expect:** B. subtilis 168 typically has a doubling time around 28–30 minutes during exponential phase under ideal conditions, calculated from the slope of the exponential growth phase. **Where to stop:** For sporulation induction: grow to an OD600 of about 0.8/mL, then move into your DSM step. **Sporulation peak:** This team used our exact strain  ![][image15]![][image16] **Materials** ·         B. subtilis 1A1 culture (harvested at OD₆₀₀ \~0.8 from LB growth curve) ·         DSM (Difco Sporulation Medium) ·         1× PBS ·         Centrifuge ·         Water bath/heat block (80°C) ·         Sterile dH₂O (serial dilutions) ·         LB agar plates (multiple per timepoint) ·         Sterile dilution tubes ·         Sampling schedule/timer  **Protocol 1-**       **Induce sporulation** Centrifuge 10 mL culture at 13,000 × g, 1 min Wash pellet with 1× PBS Resuspend in 5 mL DSM Incubate 37°C, 200 rpm → this is t=0 **2-**       **Sample at multiple timepoints** t \= 0, 4h, 8h, 12h, 16h, 20h, 24h, 36h, 48h Don't assume 24h is the peak — that's what you're testing for **3-**      **Split each sample into two branches** *Branch A — Total viable count:* Serially dilute (10⁻¹ to 10⁻⁶) Plate on LB agar Incubate overnight, 37°C Count colonies → total CFU/mL *Branch B — Heat-resistant (spore) count:* Heat-treat aliquot at 80°C for 20 min Serially dilute same way Plate on LB agar, incubate overnight Count colonies → heat-resistant CFU/mL **4-**       **Calculate sporulation efficiency** **5-**      **Plot and find the peak** \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_ ![][image17]![][image18] | [https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4248874/](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4248874/)  [https://static.igem.org/mediawiki/2016/7/74/T--UBonn\_HBRS--How-To-Bacillus-Subtilis.pdf](https://static.igem.org/mediawiki/2016/7/74/T--UBonn_HBRS--How-To-Bacillus-Subtilis.pdf)  [https://2016.igem.org/Team:Freiburg/B\_subtilis](https://2016.igem.org/Team:Freiburg/B_subtilis)   |
| Isolation of Spores [Hoor Islam](mailto:hi2151@nyu.edu) | **Isolation of Spores:  Materials:** Sterile distilled water (dH₂O) PBS **Nycodenz® (Sigma-Aldrich)** 15 mL conical centrifuge tubes Swinging-bucket centrifuge rotor Phase-contrast microscope Micropipettes and sterile tips **Method: 1\. Harvest Sporulated Culture** Transfer the sporulated culture into centrifuge tubes. Centrifuge at 3,345 × g for 20 min at 4°C. Carefully discard the supernatant. Resuspend the pellet in sterile distilled water. Repeat the wash three times, centrifuging at 3,345 × g for 20 min at 4°C between washes. **2\. Prepare Spore Suspension** Prepare 20% (w/v) Nycodenz® in PBS. Resuspend the washed pellet in the 20% Nycodenz® solution to a final concentration of approximately: 2 × 10⁹ spores/mL **3\. Prepare Density Gradient** Add 2.5 mL of 50% (w/v) Nycodenz® in PBS into a sterile 15 mL conical centrifuge tube. Carefully layer 200 μL of the spore suspension onto the surface of the 50% Nycodenz® solution without disturbing the interface. **4\. Density Gradient Centrifugation** Centrifuge the gradient using a swinging-bucket rotor at: 3,345 × g, 45 min, 4°C, Brake: OFF **5\. Recover Purified Spores** Following centrifugation, discard the upper layer containing vegetative cells and cellular debris. Carefully recover the pellet containing purified spores. **6\. Wash Purified Spores** Resuspend the pellet in sterile distilled water. Centrifuge at: 6,000 × g, 2 min, Room temperature Discard the supernatant. Repeat the wash several times (typically 3–5 washes) until all Nycodenz® is removed. **7\. Verify Spore Purity** Examine the final suspension by phase-contrast microscopy. Count the number of spores (will be seen as very bright, refractile particles under the microscope) and vegetative cells. Calculate purity using:  ![][image19] Aim for purity of approximately 99% phase-bright spores, with minimal vegetative cells or debris  **8\. Storage** Store purified spore suspensions in sterile distilled water at −20°C until use. **Biological Replicates** Prepare three independent spore populations under each experimental condition to account for biological variability. **Reviewed protocol from feedback** Spores can be purified using Nycodenz density gradient as suggested by Dr Elisa3, but because it is costly, we may look at other alternatives: Using Histodenz: Histodenz may be available to order into our lab and while it also shares the high price of Nicodenz, the good thing is a lot of papers have used it to purify their bacterial spores. We wanted to make sure we could use it for our Bacillus subtilis 168\. Heating treatment: as this paper ([https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9338887/](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9338887/)) suggests, there may be multiple ways of purifying spores. We wanted to ask if it was possible to use a quarter strength Ringer solution (like this paper ([https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC376546/](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC376546/)) did) before heating our suspension to 80 °C for 30mn to remove all vegetative cells. It is mentioned that this may damage the spores and as the test was not done on B. subtilis 168 specifically, we wanted to ask if you thought it was still possible for our strain. Lysozyme treatment: there is also the possibility for us to incubate with lysozyme at final concentrations of 1, 10, or 100 μg/mL at 4°C for 1 hr with shaking at 300 rpm (like [this paper](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9338887/) has done) after cell suspension ; we are however afraid that this may also damage the spores as well. Sonification: Finally,  [the paper](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9338887/) opted for sonication at 37 kHz at room temperature for 5 min (Elmasonic P). This is also something that makes us doubt whether the spores will remain intact.  | **1\. Isolation of spores:** Karava M, Bracharz F, Kabisch J (2019) Quantification and isolation of Bacillus subtilis spores using cell sorting and automated gating. *PLoS ONE* 14(7): e0219892. [https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0219892](https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0219892)  2\. Nicholas and Setlow Method is from the book: **Nicholson WL, Setlow P. (1990)** *"Sporulation, germination and outgrowth"* In: Harwood CR, Cutting SM (eds) **Molecular Biological Methods for Bacillus** John Wiley & Sons Ltd., Chichester, pp. 391–450  3\. Freire, Víctor, et al. “Comparative Study on the Impact of Equally Stressful Environmental Sporulation Conditions on Thermal Inactivation Kinetics of B. Subtilis Spores.” International Journal of Food Microbiology, vol. 405, no. 110349, November 2023, https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijfoodmicro.2023.110349.  |
| Freeze Drying with Sodium Alginate [Ndeye Astou Ndiaye](mailto:nn2705@nyu.edu)  | **What they did**:  encapsulated spores of Bacillus sphaericus LMG 22257 with sodium alginate using 3 techniques – extrusion, spray drying and freeze drying. Freeze drying had the highest bacterial spore survival rate (100%). **1\. Apply sporulation protocol as above (row 3).** Perform microscopic examination and endospore gram to ensure that most cells have become spores. Use Malachanite green staining (the spores will appear green while the rest of the bacteria is red). Ensure that 90% of the bacteria went through sporulation. **2\. Microencapsulation of the spores** For each sporulation technique, make a 2% weight/volume (2g of solute in 100mL of solution) spore suspension containing 106 cells/mL in 200mL of sodium alginate solution . The sodium alginate should be 2% and the final spore suspension \+ sodium alginate should be a 106 cells/mL concentration (once you have your spore concentration, use C1V1 \= C2V2 to find V1, the volume of spore suspension to use). Freeze drying:   1 \- The spore suspension \+ 2% sodium alginate is frozen at −46 °C in liquid ethanol  2 \- The mixed solution was freeze dried for 24 h in a laboratory-scale freeze  dryer (Christ Alpha 2–4/LD Plus, Germany) at a condenser temperature below 0 °C and a chamber pressure of  0.05 mbar (5 Pa).  3- The dried products in sheet form were spun to small pieces and stored in a desiccator. The viability of the spores was evaluated using a mortar specimen based test. Urea forms a yellow complex with p-dimethylaminobenzaldehyde in a low acidic solution at room temperature when hydrolyzed. Bacterial activity was thus evaluated by measuring light absorbance at 422nm by spectrophotometry. **CaCO3 precipitation** Because freeze drying has little effect on the viability of the spores, they are still able to induce CaCO3 precipitation. The samples were prepared with the addition of 2% (w/w of the cement weight) of freeze dried capsules, nutrient and calcium ion source. Then the samples are incubated in a wet-dry cycle (that simulates changes due to rain or water) in water for 7 d.  3\. Storage of the spore The strain powder form was made vacuum freeze drying and found to be optimally retained at the additive composition of 10 % skim milk powder, 4 % glucose, 4.0 % glycerol, 2 % gelatin, 4.0 % sucrose, 3 % VC, and 10.0 % trehalose in the single factor experiment. | Pungrasmi, Wiboonluk, et al. “Evaluation of Microencapsulation Techniques for MICP Bacterial Spores Applied in Self-Healing Concrete.” Scientific Reports \[London\], vol. 9, no. 1, no. 12484, August 2019, [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-019-49002-6](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-019-49002-6).   [https://www.researchgate.net/publication/287323058\_Optimization\_of\_culture\_conditions\_of\_Bacillus\_subtilis\_natto\_and\_preparation\_of\_freeze-dried\_powders\_as\_a\_potentially\_novel\_antithrombotic\_probiotic](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/287323058_Optimization_of_culture_conditions_of_Bacillus_subtilis_natto_and_preparation_of_freeze-dried_powders_as_a_potentially_novel_antithrombotic_probiotic)  |
| Germinant [Hana Elmowafy](mailto:he2161@nyu.edu)[Edward Chen](mailto:sec9064@nyu.edu) | **Germinant / Deployment finding:** B. subtilis spores can be triggered to germinate by nutrient germinants. Paper shows that L-alanine or L-valine trigger germination through the GerA receptor, while AGFK (L-asparagine, D-glucose, D-fructose, and K+) triggers germination through GerB/GerK. L-alanine is the simplest first germinant candidate, but AGFK can be a comparison condition. Germinant should be included in the deployment/carrier phase so that the spores stay dormant before application and are activated near or after spraying. Protocol from Previous Team’s Findings for Germination: Materials   Tryptone   Yeast extract   NaCl   H2Od   Agar  Becker   Measuring cylinder   DSM medium Apparatus   Neubauer Chamber   Autoclave   Digital analytical balance   Incubator   Drigalski spatula  Preparation of the solution: Sporulation:  ![][image20] Take 10 µL of the resulting solution to follow the Cell counting with Neubauer Chamber protocol. ![][image21] ![][image22]  It is used to distinguish between spores and bacteria, so that sporulation efficiency can be measured. The spore number is also important to know the germination rate. After counting, safely discard this sample.  Heat a sample until reach 120°C in an autoclave. When this temperature is reached, turn off the autoclave immediately1 . Now you have only spores alive in your solution.  Part 2 Spore germination using LB Medium (10X) 1\. Add 100 g of tryptone, 50 g of yeast extract, 100 g of NaCl and 15 g of agar in a becker;  2\. Dissolve components in 1000 mL of H2Od;  3\. Sterilize the medium by autoclaving for 20 minutes.  4\. Shed in Petri plate.  5\. To check spores viability, prepare dilutions using the protocol described in part 1\.  6\. Use 3 microtubes (1.5 mL) for each dilution. The first contains 900 μl of DSM, the second and third 990 μl each. 7\. Add 100 μl of cells (=10-1 ) and plate 100 μl (=10-2 ) to the first microtube.  8\. Add 10 μl from the 10-1 dilution to the second microtube (=10-3 ) and plate 100 μl (104 ).  9\. Add 10 μl from the 10-3 dilution to the third microtube (=10-5 ) and plate 100 μl (10-6 ).  10\. Incubate all plates at 37°C. After 12h check for colony formation periodically.  11\. Calculate the spore’s viability (concentration) following this formula: a. Viable spores/ml= amount of colonies/dilution.  12\. Comparing the result with spore concentration counted at Neubauer chamber, the germination efficiency is achieved  | Studies of the commitment step in the germination of spores of bacillus species[https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/20435722/](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/20435722/)  Bacillus subtilis – Germination Protocol  [https://static.igem.org/mediawiki/2014/a/a2/Germination\_Sporulation.pdf](https://static.igem.org/mediawiki/2014/a/a2/Germination_Sporulation.pdf)  |
| Prong 2 experiments:   b) Testing sortase activity using FRET. | Although our experiment tries to tentatively analyze our enzyme (YhcS)  activity **within** B. *subtilis* (in vivo), most papers that perform FRET on sortase do it in vitro – this is to assure that the sortase does not immediately digest the substrate and falsify the results. A simplification of the experiment is as follows: **1\. Gene design** Building the gene construct composed of: CFP-linker-LPGTS-linker-YFP and ligating it into an expression vector. Our mutant YhcS is also carried by an expression vector. **2\. Bacterial transformation and expression** The recombinant plasmids will be transformed into Escherichia coli BL21 (DE3) cells1. Note: sortase is **not** endogenous to E. coli as it is a gram negative bacteria. The resulting proteins will be purified. The 4 recombinant plasmids should be:    1- Our mutant YhcS    2- ΔYhcS (negative control)    3- LPDAS (mutant substrate for negative control of specificity)    4- There will also be a WT-YhcS for positive control. **3\. Fret activity measurement in vitro** The purified proteins are added to a FRET assay1. Then the substrate is added and the plate is incubated for 1hr at 37°C before measuring the fluorescence activity. Expected results:    1- Loss of fret (if our mutant YhcS is indeed working)    2 \- No change in FRET    3 \- No change or very minimal change  in FRET (if cleavage is specific)    4 \- Loss of FRET similar to \#1 to confirm that our YhcS is working properly Alternative: using FRET to check for anchoring as Sortase’s binding ability can be evaluated on the **presence** of FRET when the enzyme effectively binds the protein to the cell wall (if the wall has the CFP and the protein carries the YFP).4 | 1\. Zhang B, Teng Z, Li X, Lu G, Deng X, Niu X and Wang J (2017) Chalcone Attenuates Staphylococcus aureus Virulence by Targeting Sortase A and Alpha-Hemolysin. Front. Microbiol. 8:1715. doi: 10.3389/fmicb.2017.01715 [https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/microbiology/articles/10.3389/fmicb.2017.01715/full](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/microbiology/articles/10.3389/fmicb.2017.01715/full) Other papers expressing sortase through E. coli: 2\. Kinetic Mechanism of Staphylococcus aureus Sortase SrtA. Xinyi Huang, Ann Aulabaugh, Weidong Ding, Bhupesh Kapoor, Lefa Alksne, Keiko Tabei, and George Ellestad Biochemistry 2003 42 (38), 11307-11315 DOI: 10.1021/bi034391g  [https://pubs.acs.org/doi/full/10.1021/bi034391g](https://pubs.acs.org/doi/full/10.1021/bi034391g)  3\. Sortase A as a Tool to Functionalize Surfaces. Tjitske Sijbrandij, Nivedita Cukkemane, Kamran Nazmi, Enno C. I. Veerman, and Floris J. Bikker Bioconjugate Chemistry 2013 24 (5), 828-831 DOI: 10.1021/bc4000146 [https://pubs.acs.org/doi/full/10.1021/bc4000146](https://pubs.acs.org/doi/full/10.1021/bc4000146) 4\. Visualization and Quantification of Sortase Activity at the Single-Molecule Level via Transpeptidation-Directed Intramolecular Förster Resonance Energy Transfer. Yueying Li, Yong Yang, and Chun-yang Zhang Analytical Chemistry 2018 90 (21), 13007-13012 DOI: 10.1021/acs.analchem.8b03716 [https://pubs.acs.org/doi/full/10.1021/acs.analchem.8b03716](https://pubs.acs.org/doi/full/10.1021/acs.analchem.8b03716)  |
| Storage of B.subtilis short term/ long term  [Naya Mansour](mailto:nm5098@nyu.edu) |   The paper compared the storage of B,subtilis spores at different temperatures and concluded that at  4 °C, −20 °C, and −80 °C the spores were well stable and there was almost no obvious decrease of the recombinant gene after 24 months of storage (liquid or lyophilized).  Other igem team who did this:  [https://static.igem.org/mediawiki/2014/5/59/LMU\_Munich14\_Media.pdf](https://static.igem.org/mediawiki/2014/5/59/LMU_Munich14_Media.pdf) ![][image23] Here are some answers i gathered from the 3rd link on how to store bacillus subtilis spores: “Storing spores in water solutions does not prevent mold to grow while the spore suspension is kept at refrigeration temperature for months or years. An excellent medium is, even if this is counterintuitive, ethanol (\>70%). To avoid evaporation, use well sealed vials. “   ![][image24]  | [**https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/33606075/\#:\~:text=Liquid%20B.,and%20better%20at%20lower%20temperatures**](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/33606075/#:~:text=Liquid%20B.,and%20better%20at%20lower%20temperatures)**.  [https://static.igem.org/mediawiki/2014/5/59/LMU\_Munich14\_Media.pdf](https://static.igem.org/mediawiki/2014/5/59/LMU_Munich14_Media.pdf)  [https://www.researchgate.net/post/which\_is\_the\_best\_way\_to\_store\_spores\_of\_a\_Bacilluss\_culture\_Im\_thinking\_of\_using\_whatman\_filter\_and\_water\_based\_spore\_suspension](https://www.researchgate.net/post/which_is_the_best_way_to_store_spores_of_a_Bacilluss_culture_Im_thinking_of_using_whatman_filter_and_water_based_spore_suspension)  [https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7853940/](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7853940/)  [https://static.igem.org/mediawiki/2021/4/41/T--Brno\_Czech\_Republic--PROTOCOL-\_preparationglycerolstock.pdf](https://static.igem.org/mediawiki/2021/4/41/T--Brno_Czech_Republic--PROTOCOL-_preparationglycerolstock.pdf)**  |
|  Timeline of growth curves of Bacillus [Mahra Al Dhaheri](mailto:mma9420@nyu.edu) |   ![][image25]  ![][image26] The existing literature reports a typical B. subtilis doubling/generation time of roughly 28–40 minutes under favorable LB growth conditions. The iGEM manual reports 28–30 min for B. subtilis WT168, while Kadapure et al. report 40 ± 4 min for B. subtilis PSK.A2. This supports the idea that B. subtilis generally grows rapidly in rich medium, with exponential growth occurring on the scale of minutes per generation.  |  **[https://static.igem.org/mediawiki/2016/7/74/T--UBonn\_HBRS--How-To-Bacillus-Subtilis.pdf](https://static.igem.org/mediawiki/2016/7/74/T--UBonn_HBRS--How-To-Bacillus-Subtilis.pdf) [(PDF) Characterization of heat, salt, acid, alkaline, and antibiotic stress response in soil isolate Bacillus subtilis strain PSK.A2](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/381549243_Characterization_of_heat_salt_acid_alkaline_and_antibiotic_stress_response_in_soil_isolate_Bacillus_subtilis_strain_PSKA2) [https://journals.plos.org/plosone/article/file?type=printable\&id=10.1371/journal.pone.0027160](https://journals.plos.org/plosone/article/file?type=printable&id=10.1371/journal.pone.0027160)**  |
| Signal Peptide Confirmation: AmyQ [Hana Elmowafy](mailto:he2161@nyu.edu)  | Sec Export: AmyQ drives Sec-pathway secretion, which is needed before YhcR/YhcS anchoring. Established: AmyQ is already used in common *B. subtilis* secretion vectors like pHT43 AmyQ has been used successfully to secrete recombinant proteins in *B. subtilis*. No signal peptide is universally best, but AmyQ is the safest first construct; SacB/YoaW can be backups  | **1\.[https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC99003/](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC99003/) [https://link.springer.com/article/10.1186/s12934-021-01701-4](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1186/s12934-021-01701-4) 2\.[https://subtiwiki.uni-goettingen.de/wiki/index.php/PHT43](https://subtiwiki.uni-goettingen.de/wiki/index.php/PHT43) 3\.[https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5733156/](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5733156/) 4\.[https://pubs.acs.org/doi/10.1021/acs.jafc.8b04183](https://pubs.acs.org/doi/10.1021/acs.jafc.8b04183) [https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6366066/](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6366066/)**  |
| Linker choice: gene construct [Hana Elmowafy](mailto:he2161@nyu.edu)  | **Flexible linker: (GGGGS)3** Says flexible linkers are often Gly/Ser-rich and specifically discusses (GGGGS)3 as a classic flexible linker.  [https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3726540/](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3726540/) Uses (GGGGS)3 linker in fusion antibody expression and studies codon optimization within this linker. [https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/14644097/](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/14644097/) Studies Gly-Ser linkers experimentally and states that glycine-serine linkers are important components of engineered multidomain proteins [https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6150656/](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6150656/) States GS repeats are the most common linker sequence in scFv design because of their flexible nature.  [https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-022-09324-4](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-022-09324-4) States widely used flexible linkers include (GGGGS)n, (GS)n, and (G)n. [https://pubs.rsc.org/sc/article/10/44/10428/663855/Fabrication-of-rigidity-and-space-variable-protein](https://pubs.rsc.org/sc/article/10/44/10428/663855/Fabrication-of-rigidity-and-space-variable-protein) Compares structured linkers to commonly used Gly4Ser repeat linkers, supporting Gly4Ser as a standard flexible benchmark. [https://academic.oup.com/peds/article/27/10/325/1455209](https://academic.oup.com/peds/article/27/10/325/1455209) **Rigid linker: (EAAAK)3** States widely used rigid linkers include (EAAAK)n and other rigid/proline-rich linkers [https://pubs.rsc.org/sc/article/10/44/10428/663855/Fabrication-of-rigidity-and-space-variable-protein](https://pubs.rsc.org/sc/article/10/44/10428/663855/Fabrication-of-rigidity-and-space-variable-protein) States (EAAAK)n is the most common alpha-helix-forming rigid linker \*IGEM team [https://static.igem.org/mediawiki/2021/d/de/T--EPFL--contribution--linkers.pdf](https://static.igem.org/mediawiki/2021/d/de/T--EPFL--contribution--linkers.pdf) Supports helical linkers for constructing bioactive fusion proteins and protein nanostructures [https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/33482989/](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/33482989/) Uses EAAAK as the rigid elementary linker unit in a linker library, paired against flexible GGGGS units [**https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/26394862/**](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/26394862/)  |  |

# Adhesives

Option 1: Expression Alginate in *Bacillus subtilis* 

In bacterial species like *Pseudomonas aeruginosa*, sodium alginate, a biopolymer is produced. The alginate is produced through the alginate operon, which consists of [13 genes](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC2435354/). The produced alginate binds to sodium, which forms sodium alginate. Sodium alginate (SA) has been proven to be a [strong adhesive](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1875963720301154) element for sand, and is biodegradable. This alleviates environmental concerns to some extent as the chassis is already found within the microbiome of the desert, and the substance being applied is biodegradable. 

This paper highlights a tool used to transform *Bacillus subtilis* with [8 genes](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/26373726/) (an operon). The wet lab aspect/proof of concept using this method may follow this paper’s approach to slowly transforming bacteria with each new gene. Each time a gene/group of genes is added, they test for its expression, which can be our proof of concept. Though this must be approved by the PIs, I believe slowly getting more and more genes on the operon and transformed would be great proof of concept. 

Option 2: Overexpression of exopolysaccharide (EPS) and γ-PGA

This approach centers on two endogenous biopolymers of Bacillus subtilis that together form its biofilm matrix. 

The first is the exopolysaccharide produced by the epsA-O operon, **note that this is a specific polysaccharide, not to be confused with "extracellular polymeric substance," which shares the same abbreviation but refers broadly to everything bacteria secrete into their matrix.** This exopolysaccharide is the structural scaffold that holds the biofilm together and anchors cells to surfaces. It is endogenously produced and secreted, requiring no foreign export machinery. [Arnaouteli et al. (2024)](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41522-024-00555-z) found it has intrinsic viscosity comparable to xanthan gum, and [Grumbein et al. (2022)](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlelanding/2019/ra/c9ra01955c) showed that deletion mutants lacking biofilm matrix components exhibit significantly reduced erosion stability, providing direct evidence that it contributes to mechanical strength. **However**, no study has yet tested its effect on sand cohesion against wind specifically. 

The second target is poly-γ-glutamic acid (γ-PGA), a viscous, gel-forming polymer that some B. subtilis strains naturally synthesize and secrete into the extracellular space via the PgsBCA membrane complex ([Cao et al., 2022](https://doi.org/10.1128/spectrum.01329-21)),  again, no additional secretion engineering is needed. γ-PGA fills the pore spaces within the biofilm scaffold, chelates multivalent cations abundant in desert sand, and retains water. While γ-PGA has been shown to improve soil aggregate stability in desert soils ([Liang et al., 2019](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agwat.2019.03.009)) and water retention in sandy loam ([Guo et al., 2021](https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0245365)), it **also lacks a direct wind-erosion test** on sand. 

As a complementary third arm, we will engineer B. subtilis to heterologously produce **Alginate (*Option 1***), a molecule not natively made by our chassis but with proven sand-stabilization performance. This dual approach, overexpressing endogenous matrix components alongside a functionally validated heterologous molecule, gives us both biological plausibility and a performance benchmark within the same organism.

FBA (Plan to do)  
To ensure maximum biopolymer output without compromising cell viability, we plan to use Flux Balance Analysis (FBA) on a genome-scale metabolic model of *B. subtilis* to systematically identify competing pathways that divert precursors away from EPS and γ-PGA biosynthesis, as well as degradation pathways, such as the enzymes PgdS and GGT, which break down γ-PGA after secretion ([Mamberti et al., 2015](https://journals.plos.org/plosone/article?id=10.1371/journal.pone.0130810)), that could be knocked out to increase net polymer accumulation. In this way, we show excellence in synbio. 

# Carrier Solution

The carrier solution is the mixture that will be deployed on the sand by the drone/Spray system. It is a substance that has enough nutrients and the right conditions to allow for the bacteria to grow and live on the sand. It should enable B *subtilis* to induce CaCO3 precipitation in a non ureolytic pathway.

1. **Growth curve and sporulation**

From Dr Elisa Gayán:

* Critical steps: The two main points to pay attention to are avoiding cross-contamination and choosing an appropriate sporulation medium. If the sporulation medium does not support a high sporulation efficiency, a substantial proportion of vegetative cells may remain in the culture. In that case, spores should be purified from vegetative cells using density gradient centrifugation (e.g., Nycodenz).

* Negative control: spo0A mutant (unable to sporulate).

* Other factor: The main factor is ensuring that the final spore suspension is free of remaining vegetative cells.

* Specific material: Apart from standard microbiology equipment, I would recommend having Nycodenz available for density gradient purification of spores.

* Successful readout: Ideally, more than 80–90% of the cells should sporulate, and after Nycodenz purification the final suspension should contain approximately 98% pure spores. Spore purity can be verified by phase-contrast microscopy or by comparing viable counts before and after a heat pasteurization treatment, which eliminates vegetative cells while allowing spores to survive.

# Kill Switch

Toxin/anti-toxin system to prevent HGT:

- Plasmid (transferrable DNA): Holin (protein that creates pores in bacterial membrane) \+  Endosylin (enzyme that degrades cell wall)   
- Genome (not transferable): Anti-holin (a protein that blocks holin \+ prevents lysis of the cell)

In a wild type bacterium the plasmid will be transferred and not the genome so cell lysis will occur in any bacteria that receives the plasmid from our GMO by HGT. However, any bacterium that's produced from the reproduction of our GMO would obtain the plasmid and genome both so it won't undergo lysis \= self-sustaining colony

Kill switch: in desert environments, Bacillus subtilis experiences osmotic stress when water leaves the cell due to dry, high salinity conditions. The bacteria responds to the sudden environmental changes through the SigB-controlled stress response that gets activated. We could use this natural stress-sensing system to control biofilm formation so that bacteria produce a sand-binding matrix only during harsh conditions like sandstorms.   
([https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4238804/](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4238804/) \!\!)   
If we plan to make a capsule or other that has to be reapplied every once in a while just before sandstorms, a previous iGEM team worked on a kill switch for Bacillus subtilis “We tailored our kill switch for Bacillus subtilis, selecting MazF as the toxin and MazE as the antitoxin, with DRE serving as our recombinase. Additionally, we carefully chose a promoter and terminator that are compatible with Bacillus subtilis, ensuring the effectiveness of our engineered system. This kill switch is shown at BBa\_K4743030.”   
If we use this method, bacteria will form biofilm for a limited time before it dies. A separate mechanism will need to be used for biofilm production, however, we will have more control on environmental issues since the bacteria will self-eliminate after production of the biofilm.  
Another iGEM team worked on an IPTG- based proof of concept switch where bacterial survival depends on an external inducer. In the absence of IPTG, the toxin MazF is expressed and kills the cells, while in its presence, an antisense RNA blocks toxin expression and allows survival. (not adapted to environment, only labs)  

# Containment

**Containment Strategy:**

1. Deployment is in the desert where there’s gonna be less exposure to humans/animals   
2. The spores will be applied via a drone-mounted spray in stabilized strips perpendicular to the prevailing wind direction, forming "speed bumps" that reduce ground-level sand movement and limit wind-driven spore dispersal.   
3. There will be barricades/covers in the desert so people and animals don't walk on it. Something like this:

4. Kill Switch

**Deployment Strategy:**  
Location: Desert  
Barricade format:  
Need a roof, since bacteria and sand can float out of the target site even without direct wind  
Time Stamps:  
Isolation:  
0h: Place the cover \+ spray the solution (freeze-dried spores \+ water \+ supporting medium for MICP)  
Spraying and Germination:  
8h: Spray water \+ supporting medium  
16h: Spray water \+ supporting medium  
24h: Spray water \+ supporting medium  
Validation:  
32h: Spray water \+ supporting medium \+ test how solid the ground is (it should be solid enough that people walking on it don’t break it) \+ remove the cover  
\*If during testing we find out that the ground isn’t solid enough at 32h, keep spraying at 8h intervals until required solidness is achieved (it should take from 32h-72h max for the ground   
to be solid enough)

6 months: Repeat the entire spraying cycle again and keep repeating this spraying cycle every 6 months 

Why was the cover removed after 32 hours? Because the biofilm matures fully after 32 hours  
Why repeat after 6 months? Because the biofilm dies/ is no longer active after 6 months so we must renew it  
Spray System: The spores will be applied via a drone-mounted spray in stabilized strips perpendicular to the prevailing wind direction, forming "speed bumps" that reduce ground-level sand movement and limit wind-driven spore dispersal. 

**Full Containment \+ Deployment Strategy (can paste directly into wiki safety page):**  
   
The engineered *Bacillus subtilis* will be deployed as dormant spores, leveraging their natural resilience over vegetative cells. Using a drone-mounted spray system, the spores will be applied in stabilized strips perpendicular to the prevailing wind direction, forming "speed bumps" that reduce ground-level sand movement and limit wind-driven spore dispersal. Desert environments were strategically selected due to their sparse populations, reducing the risk of unintended containment breaches.  
   
Furthermore, to ensure controlled germination and stabilization, deployment will follow a strict, staged protocol. In Phase 1 (Isolation), the target deployment zone is temporarily covered, physically restricting access by humans and local wildlife. Moving into Phase 2 (Spraying & Germination), the engineered *B. subtilis* spore solution is uniformly sprayed beneath the protective cover.
